statement: llama.cpp was designed from first principles around CPU inference on commodity hardware, not as a GPU framework with CPU fallback. The architectural consequences permeate the codebase: memory-mapped GGUF model loading avoids copying weights into RAM until needed, quantized integer arithmetic replaces FP16/BF16 matmul where possible, and BLAS kernels are used for batch matrix multiplication on CPUs that support AVX2/NEON. The philosophy is that a 7B model running at 15 tok/s on a MacBook is more useful than a 70B model requiring a $10k GPU. GPU backends (Metal, CUDA, Vulkan) are acceleration layers on top of this CPU-first substrate, not the primary design target.

GGUF (GPT-Generated Unified Format) replaced GGML in August 2023 as the canonical weight format for llama.cpp. The key design decision is that GGUF is self-describing: the file header contains model architecture metadata, tokenizer vocabularies, and quantization parameters, so no external config files are needed to load a model. This is a deliberate break from PyTorch's safetensors/pickle formats which require an accompanying config.json. The implication for tooling: any code that inspects or serves GGUF models must parse the key-value metadata section of the header, not rely on sidecar files. Models distributed as GGUF are portable across llama.cpp versions (within format revision limits).

Quantization level selection involves a three-way tradeoff between size, speed, and output quality that is not linear. Q4_K_M (4-bit, K-quant, medium) is the practical sweet spot for most use cases: ~4.5 bits/weight effective, <1% perplexity regression vs FP16 on most benchmarks, fits a 7B model in ~4 GB RAM. Q5_K_S achieves lower perplexity but the size reduction over Q5_K_M is marginal. Q8_0 preserves near-FP16 quality but doubles memory vs Q4_K_M with diminishing returns. The most common failure: choosing Q2_K or Q3_K_S to maximize compression, then observing coherence collapse on long outputs and blaming the model architecture rather than quantization depth. Instruction-tuned models degrade faster at low bit depths than base models.

The KV cache for llama.cpp scales as O(n_layers * n_heads * context_length * 2 * element_size). For a 7B model at FP16 KV, a 4096-token context uses ~500 MB; a 32768-token context uses ~4 GB — often exceeding available VRAM and causing silent fallback to CPU for the KV cache, tanking throughput. The failure mode is setting --ctx-size 32768 to 'be safe' without understanding the memory cost, then observing 5x slower inference and blaming model quantization. Mitigations: --flash-attn reduces KV memory significantly; --cache-type-k q8_0 and --cache-type-v q8_0 quantize the KV cache itself at minor quality cost. Correctly sizing context to actual task needs is more impactful than model selection.

llama.cpp supports three GPU backends with different build requirements and performance profiles. Metal (macOS/Apple Silicon) is the default on Mac and is compiled into the standard Homebrew/binary builds — no extra flags needed. CUDA requires building with LLAMA_CUDA=ON and a working CUDA toolkit; it provides the highest throughput on NVIDIA hardware. Vulkan is the cross-platform GPU path for AMD/Intel/non-CUDA NVIDIA on Linux/Windows; it avoids the CUDA toolchain but has ~10-20% overhead vs CUDA on equivalent hardware. The --n-gpu-layers flag controls how many transformer layers offload to GPU; setting it to 999 offloads all layers. Mixing backends (e.g., some layers on CPU, some on Metal) is supported and useful when VRAM is insufficient for the full model.

llama.cpp implements grammar-constrained decoding via GBNF (a variant of BNF grammars) applied at the token logit level during sampling. The constraint is enforced by masking logits for tokens that would violate the grammar at each generation step — not by post-processing or rejection sampling. The practical effect: you can guarantee JSON output, structured enums, or regex-like patterns with zero hallucination of format, at the cost of slightly slower sampling (logit masking is O(vocab_size) per step). The failure mode is writing grammars that are too restrictive and cause the sampler to get stuck (all logits masked), producing an infinite loop. The llama.cpp repo ships a json.gbnf grammar as a reference; testing grammars against your actual model before production is mandatory.

llama.cpp offers two integration surfaces: the llama-server binary (OpenAI-compatible HTTP API) and the libllama C library (direct embedding). Server mode is the correct choice for most application developers: it handles batching, concurrent requests, KV cache sharing across requests, and exposes /completion, /chat/completions, and /embeddings endpoints. Library mode (via Python bindings like llama-cpp-python) is appropriate when you need fine-grained control over sampling, custom logit processors, or single-process latency without HTTP overhead. A common anti-pattern is using llama-cpp-python in a web service and reinventing request queuing — the server binary already implements this correctly with an --parallel flag for concurrent slots.

llama.cpp's rope_freq_scale and rope_freq_base parameters must match the values the model was trained with for long-context inference to work correctly. Models trained with RoPE scaling (e.g., Llama 3.1 trained with rope_theta=500000 for 128K context) require --rope-freq-base 500000 when running inference beyond the model's original training context. Omitting this flag with a long-context model and high --ctx-size produces coherent-looking but factually degraded output: the model processes the input but position encodings for tokens beyond the original training context are wrong, causing the model to effectively 'ignore' or misattribute distant context. The failure is silent — the model outputs text without errors, but quality degrades with distance from the current position in ways that are hard to detect without ground truth.

llama.cpp's batch processing mode (llama_batch API or --batch-size flag in the server) determines how many tokens are processed in a single forward pass during prefill. A larger batch size increases prefill throughput by allowing the matrix multiplications to be more efficiently parallelized on CPU or GPU, but increases peak memory usage proportionally. The default --batch-size 512 is conservative for GPU inference; raising it to 2048 or 4096 can reduce prefill time by 2-4x for long prompts on GPU. On CPU, the optimal batch size depends on the L2/L3 cache size of the processor — too large a batch causes cache misses that eliminate the parallelism benefit. For Apple Silicon (Metal), --batch-size 2048 with --ubatch-size 512 (physical sub-batch size for Metal compute shaders) is the recommended starting point per the llama.cpp performance guide.

llama.cpp implements mirostat sampling (--mirostat 1 or --mirostat 2) as an alternative to temperature+top_p sampling. Mirostat dynamically adjusts the token selection threshold to maintain a target 'surprise' level (perplexity) per token, controlled by the --mirostat-tau parameter. The practical benefit: mirostat produces more consistent output quality across diverse prompts compared to fixed temperature settings because it adapts to the model's confidence distribution at each step. A fixed temperature=0.7 produces incoherent tokens when the model is uncertain (high-entropy positions) and overly conservative tokens when the model is confident (low-entropy positions). For creative writing tasks with variable complexity, mirostat-2 with tau=5.0 is empirically preferred by the llama.cpp community over temperature sampling. Mirostat is most useful when the prompt distribution is diverse; for narrow-domain tasks, temperature+top_p is simpler and comparably effective.

llama.cpp's Python bindings (llama-cpp-python) have a separate version lifecycle from the underlying C++ library and are often 2-4 releases behind. The package ships pre-compiled wheels for common platforms, but CUDA and Metal acceleration require either using the correct pre-built wheel variant (llama-cpp-python[cuda], llama-cpp-python[metal]) or building from source with the appropriate CMAKE_ARGS. The failure pattern: installing the default llama-cpp-python wheel and getting CPU-only inference without any warning, even on a GPU machine. The diagnostic is checking the output of `from llama_cpp import llama_supports_gpu_offload; print(llama_supports_gpu_offload())`. Failing to include CUDA support in a Docker image's build step is a common CI/CD oversight that causes production inference to be 10-50x slower than tested locally on GPU hardware.

llama.cpp's prompt caching (--prompt-cache) saves the KV state after processing a prompt prefix to a binary file on disk. On subsequent runs with the same prefix, the cache is loaded and processing resumes from the saved state, eliminating re-computation of the prefix. This is particularly valuable for benchmark evaluation scripts that repeat the same system prompt thousands of times, and for interactive applications where users start conversations with the same multi-thousand-token context. The cache is prefix-keyed: the saved state is valid only for prompts that start with exactly the same bytes as the cached prefix. Cache invalidation is manual — there is no TTL or automatic staleness detection. Changing the model, quantization, or context size invalidates the cache but does not delete the file; loading a stale cache file silently produces incorrect results, often manifesting as degraded output quality.

llama.cpp's --threads and --threads-batch flags control CPU parallelism for token generation and prompt processing respectively. The optimal --threads value is NOT the total CPU core count — it is the number of physical cores on a single NUMA node. Using all logical cores (including hyperthreaded siblings) for a single llama.cpp process typically reduces throughput because matrix-vector multiplication in token generation is memory-bandwidth-bound, not compute-bound, and hyperthreading adds context-switching overhead on memory-bandwidth-saturated operations. The empirical recommendation: set --threads to the physical core count of one NUMA node (check with `lscpu | grep 'Core(s) per socket'`). On a 16-core/32-thread AMD EPYC, --threads 8 (one NUMA node's cores) typically outperforms --threads 32 (all logical cores) for single-model inference.

llama.cpp's speculative decoding (--draft-model or --draft flag in server mode) requires the draft model to use the same tokenizer vocabulary as the target model. Unlike vLLM's stricter check, llama.cpp accepts mismatched tokenizers at load time and only fails when the draft produces a token ID that the target's vocabulary cannot decode — which manifests as garbled token in the output stream rather than an error. A subtler failure: even with matching vocabulary, if the draft model has significantly different token probability distributions (e.g., a draft fine-tuned on code paired with a target tuned for chat), acceptance rates drop below 50%, making speculative decoding slower than direct generation due to verification overhead. Speculative decoding only improves throughput when the draft model has ≥70% acceptance rate on your actual workload; measure acceptance rate before deploying to production.

llama.cpp's model format conversion pipeline (convert_hf_to_gguf.py) transforms HuggingFace safetensors or PyTorch .bin models to GGUF format before quantization. The conversion step must happen before quantization (using llama-quantize), not after — GGUF is the input format for quantization, not an output of it. Common workflow confusion: developers try to quantize a HuggingFace model directly and get a format error. The correct sequence is: (1) download HF model, (2) run convert_hf_to_gguf.py to get an F16 GGUF, (3) run llama-quantize on the F16 GGUF to get Q4_K_M or desired quantization. The conversion script requires the model's tokenizer files (tokenizer.json, tokenizer_config.json) to be present alongside the weight files — conversion without tokenizer produces a GGUF that loads but generates garbage because the vocab mapping is missing.

llama.cpp is implemented in pure C/C++ with zero mandatory runtime dependencies, enabling deployment on any platform from Raspberry Pi to cloud servers without installing Python, CUDA toolkits, or other language runtimes. The design prioritizes portability over convenience: a single statically-linked binary can serve inference on ARM, x86, RISC-V, and WebAssembly targets. This approach allows embedding LLM inference in existing C/C++ applications, mobile apps, and embedded systems where Python is unavailable or too heavy. The trade-off is that the API surface is lower-level than Python frameworks, requiring more boilerplate for common tasks like prompt templating and chat formatting.

llama.cpp's GGUF format encodes quantization type, model architecture, and tensor layout in a single file using a binary format with KV metadata header followed by tensor data. When loading a GGUF file, llama.cpp validates magic bytes (GGUF), version number, and architecture metadata against supported model types. If the GGUF was produced by a newer version of llama.cpp with features the current binary does not support, loading fails with a version mismatch error. Users must keep gguf-py and llama.cpp in sync when converting models; using gguf-py 0.9 to convert a model then trying to load with llama.cpp built from a 3-month-old commit frequently causes failures due to new metadata fields added for features like context scaling.

llama.cpp with Metal backend on Apple Silicon fails silently when the model exceeds the unified memory budget: inference continues but all computations fall back to CPU with no warning message, resulting in 10-50x slower performance. The failure mode is insidious because llama.cpp does not abort or log a Metal allocation failure — it simply routes tensors to CPU. Symptom is tokens/sec dropping from 50+ (GPU) to 2-5 (CPU). Diagnosis: run llama-cli with --n-gpu-layers verbose logging or use Metal Frame Debugger to observe GPU memory allocation. Fix: either reduce n_ctx, use a smaller quantization (Q4_K_M instead of Q8_0), or explicitly set --n-gpu-layers 0 to confirm CPU baseline then incrementally increase.

statement: Converting a HuggingFace model to GGUF format requires: (1) Clone llama.cpp repo and install Python deps: pip install -r requirements/requirements-convert_hf_to_gguf.txt. (2) Run python convert_hf_to_gguf.py /path/to/hf_model --outfile model-f16.gguf --outtype f16 to produce a full-precision base. (3) Quantize: ./llama-quantize model-f16.gguf model-q4_k_m.gguf Q4_K_M. (4) Validate: ./llama-cli -m model-q4_k_m.gguf -p 'Hello' --n-predict 10 to confirm output. Key pitfalls: some model architectures (Falcon, StarCoder2) require architecture-specific convert scripts; tokenizer config must be present in the HF model directory; models with custom code (trust_remote_code=True) may fail conversion and need manual tensor mapping patches.

llama.cpp implements speculative decoding via the llama-speculative binary, enabling 2-4x speedup for deterministic outputs using a small draft model that proposes token sequences verified by the main model. The draft model must share vocabulary and tokenizer with the main model. Common pairing: Llama-3.1-8B-Q4_K_M as draft for Llama-3.1-70B-Q4_K_M as main. Configuration: --model-draft path/to/draft.gguf --draft-max 8 --draft-min 5. The speedup depends on output predictability: code generation achieves near 4x while open-ended creative writing achieves only 1.5x. Speculative decoding also works with the llama-server OpenAI-compatible endpoint by passing draft_model in the server startup config.

A private document Q&A system with llama.cpp can be assembled without any Python framework: (1) Use llama-embedding to generate embeddings for document chunks stored in SQLite with vector extension (sqlite-vss). (2) At query time, embed the user question with llama-embedding, find top-k chunks via cosine similarity SQL query, concatenate into a prompt. (3) Pass assembled prompt to llama-server REST API (POST /completion). (4) Stream the response back to the user interface. The entire pipeline runs in a single Docker container with no external dependencies. Key parameter: --ctx-size 8192 minimum to accommodate long retrieved contexts. This pattern supports air-gapped environments where no internet access is allowed.

statement: llama.cpp's CUDA backend fails to compile on systems where CUDA toolkit version mismatches the installed GPU driver. The cmake build system detects CUDA via find_package(CUDAToolkit) but does not validate driver compatibility — if the CUDA toolkit is 12.4 but the driver only supports up to CUDA 12.1, compilation succeeds but runtime fails with CUDA_ERROR_INVALID_VALUE when allocating GPU memory. This is particularly common on enterprise systems where the driver is locked to a specific version by IT policy. Diagnostic: run nvidia-smi to check driver CUDA support level, then verify cmake -DLLAMA_CUDA=ON .. output shows matching toolkit version. Fix: use CMAKE_CUDA_ARCHITECTURES to target the specific GPU compute capability, and pin CUDA toolkit to the highest version the driver supports.

statement: Continuous batching with llama-server enables handling multiple simultaneous requests efficiently by processing tokens from different sequences in parallel within a single forward pass. Enable with --parallel N (number of simultaneous sequences) and --cont-batching flags. Unlike naive sequential processing, continuous batching fills GPU compute to capacity: while one sequence is at the decode phase, another can be prefilling. Monitoring endpoint GET /health and GET /metrics (Prometheus format) expose queue depth, tokens/sec, and sequence utilization. Production configuration: set --parallel 4-8 for consumer GPUs, --parallel 16-32 for A100/H100. Combined with --mlock to prevent model swapping from RAM to disk, this pattern achieves near-100% GPU utilization for sustained request loads.

llama-server exposes an OpenAI-compatible REST API at /v1/chat/completions, /v1/completions, and /v1/embeddings endpoints. The chat completions endpoint accepts the same JSON schema as OpenAI's API: {model, messages, temperature, max_tokens, stream, tools}. Tool calling is supported via Jinja2 chat templates embedded in GGUF metadata for models that include them (Llama-3.1, Mistral Nemo). Additional llama.cpp-specific extensions: cache_prompt boolean for KV cache reuse across requests with shared prefixes, and samplers array for fine-grained sampling chain control. Authentication: --api-key flag enables bearer token auth compatible with OpenAI SDK's api_key parameter.

llama.cpp's grammar-constrained generation (JSON Schema via GBNF grammars) fails to complete when the schema contains recursive structures or deeply nested arrays. The GBNF grammar compiler converts JSON Schema to a context-free grammar, but recursive schemas like {type: object, properties: {children: {type: array, items: {$ref: '#'}}}} cause infinite grammar expansion loops that hang the server. This is a known limitation: llama.cpp's GBNF does not support recursive grammar rules. Workaround: flatten the schema (replace recursive refs with a fixed maximum nesting depth), or use two-pass generation (generate free text first, then parse and validate with a separate JSON parser). For production use, constrained decoding libraries like outlines or guidance that support recursive grammars are more robust.

llama.cpp uses a KV cache architecture where the key-value attention states for all processed tokens are stored in a contiguous memory block allocated at startup based on n_ctx and n_batch parameters. Pre-allocating the full KV cache avoids fragmentation and enables constant-time cache management, which is critical for real-time inference latency. The downside is that n_ctx × n_layers × 2 × head_dim × sizeof(float16) bytes are reserved even if not used — a 4096 context, 32 layers, 128 head dim model reserves 4096 × 32 × 2 × 128 × 2 = 67MB just for KV cache. For memory-constrained devices, reducing n_ctx is the most effective way to reduce memory beyond quantization because the KV cache scales linearly with context length while model weights are fixed size.

statement: Running llama.cpp inference on ROCm (AMD GPU) requires: (1) Install ROCm 5.7+ and verify with rocm-smi. (2) Set AMDGPU target: export HSA_OVERRIDE_GFX_VERSION=11.0.0 for RDNA3 or 10.3.0 for RDNA2. (3) Build with cmake -DLLAMA_HIPBLAS=ON -DAMDGPU_TARGETS=gfx1100 .. (for RX 7900 XTX). (4) Verify GPU allocation: llama-cli --n-gpu-layers 99 should show 'using HIP device'. Gotchas: ROCm support lags CUDA by 1-2 months; gfx1030 (RX 6000 series) needs different AMDGPU_TARGETS; Instinct MI300X uses gfx942. The most common failure is HSA_OVERRIDE_GFX_VERSION not being set for newer RDNA3 GPUs that ROCm doesn't yet recognize, causing all computation to fall back to CPU.

llama.cpp's llama_eval function is not thread-safe when sharing a single llama_context between threads. The context holds mutable state including the KV cache write head position, sampling state, and output logits buffer. Concurrent calls from multiple threads corrupt these shared state structures, producing garbage output or segfaults. The correct pattern for multi-threaded serving is either: (1) one context per thread (high memory usage), or (2) use llama-server which handles concurrency internally with a request queue and per-slot context management. The llama.cpp C++ API also provides llama_new_context_with_model allowing multiple contexts to share the same immutable model weights while maintaining independent KV caches — this is the memory-efficient multi-tenant pattern.

statement: llama.cpp supports fill-in-the-middle (FIM) generation for code completion, compatible with models trained with the SPM (suffix-prefix-middle) or PSM token order. Supported models include DeepSeek-Coder, StarCoder2, and CodeLlama. To use FIM: construct a prompt with <PRE>{prefix}<SUF>{suffix}<MID> tokens (model-specific special tokens) and pass it to /v1/completions. The llama-server also exposes /infill endpoint accepting {prefix, suffix} JSON that automatically constructs the FIM prompt using the model's chat template. FIM generation achieves near-human-level single-function code completion for well-represented languages (Python, JavaScript, Go) at Q4_K_M quantization with negligible quality loss versus full-precision.

llama.cpp prompt processing performance degrades severely when the prompt contains more tokens than n_batch (default 512): the prefill phase processes tokens in chunks of n_batch, and each chunk requires a separate forward pass. With a 32K token document and n_batch=512, prefill takes 63 forward passes versus 1 for a native transformer that processes the full sequence in parallel. Fix: compile with -DLLAMA_CUDA=ON and set --batch-size 4096 (or the GPU VRAM budget allows) to process larger token chunks per pass. For CPU-only inference, increasing n_batch beyond CPU L3 cache size (typically 8-32MB) yields diminishing returns due to cache thrash. Monitor prefill speed with --verbose flag: tokens/ms during prefill versus decode shows the imbalance.

statement: Prompt caching with llama-server's KV cache reuse is enabled via the cache_prompt parameter in /v1/completions requests. When consecutive requests share a common prefix (system prompt, few-shot examples), the server detects the matching prefix and skips recomputing attention for those tokens. For a 2000-token system prompt plus 100-token user message, prompt caching reduces prefill cost by ~95% on the second request. Implementation: the server stores a hash of each request's prompt prefix; on new requests, it finds the longest cached prefix match and starts computation from that position. Critical: prompt caching only works when requests arrive to the same server instance, and the cache is cleared when the server restarts or when --cont-batching causes cache eviction under load.

statement: A code review assistant pipeline with llama.cpp: (1) llama-server starts with CodeLlama-34B-Instruct Q4_K_M, --n-gpu-layers 99, --ctx-size 16384, --parallel 2. (2) A Python wrapper reads git diff output and chunks it by file into segments <8K tokens. (3) For each chunk, POST to /v1/chat/completions with a system prompt for code review and the diff as user message. (4) Responses are aggregated into a structured report with file-by-file findings. (5) A final summarization pass combines all findings. Key configuration: set temperature 0.1 for consistent reviews, top_p 0.9, repeat_penalty 1.1 to prevent repetitive output. This pipeline processes a 500-line diff in ~45 seconds on RTX 3090, producing actionable review comments comparable to senior developer feedback for well-represented languages.

llama.cpp's mmap-based model loading fails on Docker containers when the container's shared memory (/dev/shm) is smaller than the model file. By default Docker limits /dev/shm to 64MB, but llama.cpp uses mmap to map the full GGUF file into virtual address space — on Linux kernels this operation goes through /dev/shm for certain memory-mapped operations. Symptom: model loading hangs indefinitely or produces 'Cannot allocate memory' errors for models >64MB. Fix: add --shm-size 16g (or appropriate model size) to docker run command, or build with -DLLAMA_NO_MMAP=ON to use explicit read() instead of mmap (trades startup speed for compatibility). Kubernetes users must set resources.limits.memory to at least 2x model file size.

llama.cpp supports multimodal inference for vision-language models through the llava architecture integration (llava-cli and llama-server with --mmproj flag). Supported models: LLaVA 1.5, LLaVA 1.6 (LLaVA-NeXT), BakLLaVA, and Obsidian. The multimodal projector (clip model) is a separate GGUF file loaded alongside the base language model GGUF. Images are encoded as CLIP embeddings and injected into the token sequence at the <image> placeholder token position. REST API: POST /v1/chat/completions with messages containing image_url type content blocks. Performance: image encoding takes ~200ms on GPU, then text generation proceeds at normal speed. Maximum image resolution depends on the model's patch size; LLaVA 1.6 supports up to 4 tiles of 336×336 for a maximum effective resolution of 1344×336.

llama.cpp's quantized models have a quality floor below which task performance degrades unacceptably: Q2_K (2-bit) quantization reduces model size by ~75% versus F16 but introduces 15-25% task accuracy degradation on reasoning benchmarks for 7B models. Q4_K_M is the community-accepted minimum for production use, balancing 4.5 bits/weight average with <5% quality loss on most benchmarks. Q8_0 is essentially lossless (<1% degradation) but only 2x smaller than F16. For models >30B parameters, lower quantizations (Q3_K_M) become viable because the absolute number of weights compensates for per-weight precision loss. The rule of thumb: use the largest quantization your hardware can load with n_ctx=4096, because context length matters more than quantization level for most production tasks.

Deploying llama-server as a systemd service for production use: (1) Build llama-server binary and copy to /usr/local/bin/llama-server. (2) Copy model file to /opt/models/. (3) Create /etc/systemd/system/llama-server.service with ExecStart=/usr/local/bin/llama-server -m /opt/models/llama-3.1-8b-q4_k_m.gguf --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8080 --n-gpu-layers 99 --ctx-size 4096 --parallel 4 --cont-batching and Restart=always. (4) systemctl daemon-reload && systemctl enable --now llama-server. (5) Verify: curl http://localhost:8080/health. For security: set --api-key $(openssl rand -hex 32) in the service file and store the key in /etc/llama-server.env referenced via EnvironmentFile=. Add ufw rule: ufw allow from 10.0.0.0/8 to any port 8080 for LAN-only access.

llama.cpp's temperature=0 (greedy decoding) does not produce fully deterministic output across different hardware platforms or batch sizes due to floating-point non-associativity in parallel reduction operations. When --parallel > 1, the order of floating-point additions in attention computation varies with batch fill, producing subtly different logit values at the 1e-6 level. This causes different greedy selections at token positions where multiple tokens have very similar log-probabilities. For reproducibility-critical applications (test suites, benchmarks), the only reliable fix is: --parallel 1 (single sequence), --seed with fixed value, and compile with -DLLAMA_NATIVE=OFF to disable architecture-specific floating-point optimizations. Even then, CPU vs GPU will produce different outputs due to different FP32/FP16 math paths.

Embedding generation with llama-embedding (or llama-server /v1/embeddings) should use --pooling mean or --pooling cls depending on model training. Models trained with mean pooling (most sentence-transformers, nomic-embed-text) produce better embeddings when pooled over all token positions. Models trained with CLS token pooling (BERT-style) use the first token's representation. Using the wrong pooling type silently produces lower-quality embeddings without any error — similarity scores between semantically related texts drop 20-40%. Always check the model card. For production retrieval systems, validate embedding quality by computing cosine similarity between a known positive pair (question + correct answer): values <0.6 suggest wrong pooling or wrong model.

llama.cpp quantizes weights using a block-based approach (GGML quantization) where every 32 weights share a scale factor, rather than per-tensor or per-channel quantization. Block quantization provides a middle ground: it captures weight distribution variation better than global scale factors (less quality loss than pure int4) while being simpler and faster than per-channel approaches (no extra memory access patterns). The Q4_K_M format specifically uses a 4-bit quantization with two types of blocks — a 'super-block' that contains multiple 'blocks' sharing a half-precision scale, enabling adaptive precision allocation. This hierarchical quantization is why Q4_K_M quality is noticeably better than a naive Q4_0 quantization at the same bit budget.

llama.cpp's LoRA adapter support via --lora flag fails silently when the adapter was trained with a different base model version than the loaded GGUF. LoRA adapters encode rank-factored weight deltas keyed to specific layer names and shapes; if the base model was updated (e.g., Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct v1.0 → v1.1 with modified attention head count), the adapter loads without error but produces nonsensical output because weight shapes don't match. Detection: run inference with and without the adapter using a known prompt and compare perplexity — if perplexity is higher with the adapter, the adapter is incompatible. Prevention: always store the exact base model commit hash alongside adapter weights; use --lora-scaled to reduce adapter strength as a diagnostic to see if output improves.

llama.cpp's llama-bench tool provides systematic performance benchmarking across quantization types, context lengths, and batch sizes, outputting results in markdown, CSV, or JSON format. Usage: ./llama-bench -m model.gguf -p '512,1024,2048' -n '128,512' -b '1,4,16' measures prompt processing (pp) and token generation (tg) throughput at each combination. The tool runs each configuration 5 times by default and reports mean ± stddev in tokens/sec. This enables data-driven hardware sizing decisions: benchmarking Q4_K_M vs Q8_0 on a target GPU shows whether the 2x size difference justifies the quality improvement for a given latency budget. Results are reproducible to within 2-3% variance for GPU-bound workloads.

llama.cpp cannot load models that exceed the physical RAM (or VRAM for GPU layers) because model weights are loaded into memory before inference begins, unlike some frameworks that support weight streaming. A 70B model at Q4_K_M requires ~40GB of memory; attempting to load on a system with 32GB RAM causes OOM during model loading, not during inference. Memory planning: calculate required memory as (num_parameters × bits_per_weight / 8) + KV_cache_size + ~1GB overhead. For multi-GPU setups, use --tensor-split to distribute weights across GPUs (e.g., --tensor-split 0.5,0.5 for equal split across two equal GPUs). CPU-GPU split loading (--n-gpu-layers N with N < total layers) allows partial GPU acceleration when the model doesn't fully fit in VRAM, at the cost of PCIe bandwidth for the CPU layers.

evidence_refs.excerpt: Memory requirements and multi-GPU tensor splitting

statement: Structured output enforcement via JSON mode in llama-server: pass grammar parameter in the request body with a GBNF grammar string that enforces valid JSON. For common use cases, llama-server ships pre-built grammars in the grammars/ directory: json.gbnf for arbitrary JSON, json_arr.gbnf for JSON arrays. For schema-specific outputs, generate a custom GBNF from a JSON Schema using the grammar-from-json-schema binary. Example: ./grammar-from-json-schema < schema.json > output.gbnf, then use that grammar string in API requests. This approach guarantees syntactically valid output but not semantically correct output — the model still decides field values, so a grammar for {name: string, age: integer} can produce {name: 'Alice', age: -9999}. For semantic validation, post-process responses with Pydantic.

evidence_refs.excerpt: GBNF grammar for structured output enforcement

statement: llama.cpp's perplexity evaluation (./llama-perplexity) consumes significantly more memory than inference because it processes the full test corpus in a single context window and stores all logits. A 512MB model at Q4_K_M can require 4-8GB during perplexity evaluation on a standard 512-token sliding window test. On systems close to memory limits, perplexity evaluation crashes with OOM while normal inference succeeds. Workaround: use --ppl-stride to increase the sliding window step size (reducing memory by processing fewer overlapping windows) or reduce --ctx-size during evaluation. Note that perplexity computed with different stride values is not directly comparable to published benchmarks that use standard evaluation setups.

A local transcription + summarization pipeline using llama.cpp: (1) whisper.cpp (sister project) transcribes audio to text via ./whisper-cli -m models/ggml-large-v3.bin -f audio.mp3 -otxt. (2) The transcript is passed to llama-server as a summarization request via POST /v1/chat/completions with the transcript in the user message. (3) Response is structured as meeting notes with action items using a JSON grammar. Both whisper.cpp and llama.cpp share the GGML computation library, allowing coexistence in the same Docker container. Memory allocation: whisper.cpp releases GPU memory after transcription before llama.cpp loads, preventing VRAM exhaustion. This pipeline processes a 1-hour meeting recording in ~5 minutes on RTX 3090.

llama.cpp's Windows build via MSVC fails to correctly handle UTF-8 encoded source files when the Windows console code page is not set to UTF-8 (CP65001). String literals containing non-ASCII characters in model name metadata or error messages may be corrupted, and file paths with non-ASCII characters (common in East Asian locales) cause model loading failures. The root cause is Windows' default ANSI code page (CP1252 for Western Europe) being applied to file paths. Fix: add manifest to binary with UTF-8 encoding support, set ActiveCodePage=UTF-8 in Windows 10+ manifest, and call SetConsoleOutputCP(65001) early in main(). The CMake build on Windows via MSYS2/MinGW typically handles this better than pure MSVC builds.

llama.cpp's llama-server supports WebSocket-based streaming at /v1/chat/completions with stream=true, enabling real-time token delivery to browser frontends without polling. The response is Server-Sent Events (SSE) format with data: {choices:[{delta:{content:'token'}}]} events and a final data: [DONE] event, matching OpenAI's streaming API exactly. Browser clients can use EventSource API or fetch with ReadableStream to consume the SSE stream. Combined with llama-server's --origins '*' CORS flag, this enables building browser-based chatbots that connect directly to a locally running llama-server — useful for intranet knowledge base tools where the browser is on the same network as the inference server.

llama.cpp's IQ (importance quantization) formats (IQ1_S, IQ2_XS, IQ3_S) use activation-based importance scores to selectively preserve high-importance weights at higher precision while aggressively quantizing low-importance weights. While IQ formats achieve better perplexity per bit than K-quants, they require a separate importance matrix file (imatrix) computed from a calibration dataset. If the imatrix was computed with a domain-mismatched calibration set (e.g., Wikipedia text used to create imatrix for a code model), quality degradation is worse than standard K-quants. Users must either compute a domain-appropriate imatrix or use K-quant formats. The imatrix computation requires the full F16 model and ~100 calibration samples, taking 30-60 minutes on GPU.

Fine-tuning with llama.cpp's llama-finetune (LoRA training) requires: (1) Prepare training data as a single text file with examples separated by the model's EOS token. (2) Run: ./llama-finetune --model-base base.gguf --lora-out lora.gguf --train-data train.txt --epochs 3 --batch 4 --ctx 512 --rope-freq-base 10000. (3) Merge adapter: ./llama-export-lora --model-base base.gguf --lora lora.gguf --output merged.gguf. (4) Test merged model. Practical constraints: llama-finetune only supports LoRA fine-tuning (not full fine-tuning), recommended for adapting style and format rather than injecting factual knowledge; GPU memory requirement is ~3x inference memory due to optimizer states; fine-tuning on consumer GPUs is limited to 7B models with ctx≤512.

llama.cpp uses SIMD intrinsics (AVX2, AVX512, ARM NEON, SVE) for CPU matrix multiplication, which is the dominant computation in transformer inference. At compile time, CMake detects available ISA extensions and enables corresponding code paths. SIMD quantized matrix multiplication (GGML_TYPE_Q4_K × float32) achieves 4-8x speedup over scalar code on modern x86 CPUs by processing 8-16 weights in parallel per instruction. The build flag -DLLAMA_NATIVE=ON enables additional compiler optimizations for the exact CPU being compiled on, yielding 10-20% additional speedup but making the binary non-portable to older CPU generations. For distribution, use -DLLAMA_NATIVE=OFF and rely on runtime dispatch to select the best available SIMD code path.

evidence_refs.excerpt: SIMD optimization flags for CPU inference

statement: llama.cpp model loading hangs indefinitely on NFS-mounted model files due to mmap behavior on network filesystems. NFS does not support the MAP_POPULATE flag that llama.cpp uses to pre-fault pages during mmap, causing the initial page fault to wait for NFS roundtrips. On a 10GB model file over a 1Gbps NFS link, this can cause a 90-second+ load time that appears as a hang. Fix: either copy the model to local SSD before loading, or build with -DLLAMA_NO_MMAP=ON to use sequential read() instead of mmap (slower cold start on SSD but consistent on NFS). Alternatively, pre-load the file to OS cache with: cat model.gguf > /dev/null before starting llama-server.

Implementing a semantic search service with llama.cpp as the embedding backend: use llama-server in embedding mode (--embedding flag, no --n-predict needed) behind a simple HTTP proxy that maintains a connection pool. Client code sends POST /v1/embeddings with batches of texts (up to --batch-size tokens total). Store embeddings in Qdrant or pgvector. For the retrieval path, embed queries with the same llama-server instance. Key optimization: batch embedding requests — sending 50 texts in one API call is 40x faster than 50 individual calls due to GPU utilization from batching. Model selection: nomic-embed-text-v1.5 GGUF (137M parameters) achieves MTEB benchmark scores competitive with OpenAI text-embedding-3-small at 1/10th the cost for on-premises deployment.

llama.cpp's temperature sampling with repeat_penalty fails to prevent the model from generating infinite repetition loops when temperature is very low (<0.1) and repeat_penalty is applied over a small last_n_tokens window (default 64). With low temperature, the model's probability distribution is sharply peaked, and repeat_penalty multiplicatively reduces the probabilities of seen tokens. However, once the model enters a repetitive state, it generates tokens that weren't in the last 64-token window, so they receive full probability despite being part of the larger repetition pattern. Fix: increase --repeat-last-n 256 or more to cover the full repetition cycle length, or use --presence-penalty in addition to --repeat-penalty to penalize all previously seen tokens regardless of position.

llama.cpp's Vulkan backend (build with cmake -DLLAMA_VULKAN=ON) enables GPU acceleration on any GPU with Vulkan support, including AMD, Intel, NVIDIA, and even some mobile GPUs via Vulkan drivers. This makes it the most portable GPU backend, supporting hardware that CUDA and Metal don't cover (AMD on Windows without ROCm, Intel Arc GPUs, older AMD cards). Performance is typically 60-80% of native CUDA/Metal due to Vulkan's more general-purpose compute model. For development and testing on non-standard GPU hardware, the Vulkan backend is the recommended fallback. On Intel Arc: cmake -DLLAMA_VULKAN=ON -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release .. achieves ~15 tokens/sec for 7B Q4_K_M on Arc A770, making it viable for development workloads.

evidence_refs.excerpt: Vulkan GPU backend for cross-platform acceleration

statement: llama.cpp's --rope-scaling and --rope-freq-base flags for context extension (YaRN, linear scaling) only work correctly when they match the values used during the model's training or fine-tuning. Using --rope-freq-base 500000 (Llama 3.1's extended context value) on a base Llama 2 model that was trained with --rope-freq-base 10000 enables inference beyond the training context length but produces significant quality degradation because the model has not learned to handle the modified positional encodings. Context extension only improves quality when applied to a model that was fine-tuned with the same RoPE parameters. Check the GGUF metadata field rope.freq_base to verify the model's actual training value before overriding.

A privacy-preserving customer support chatbot with llama.cpp: (1) llama-server runs Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct Q4_K_M with --api-key, --ssl-key-file, --ssl-cert-file for HTTPS, firewalled to internal network only. (2) A thin Next.js proxy layer handles session management, input sanitization, and PII detection (email/phone regex filtering before sending to llama-server). (3) System prompt encodes product knowledge base as static context (2000 tokens) cached with cache_prompt=true. (4) Conversation history maintained server-side with 10-turn rolling window. (5) Output filtered through profanity and off-topic classifiers. All customer PII stays on-premise; no data leaves the network. Architecture enables GDPR compliance without relying on third-party LLM APIs that require DPA agreements.

llama.cpp's tensor offloading to GPU (--n-gpu-layers) causes slowdowns rather than speedups when the PCIe bandwidth between CPU and GPU becomes the bottleneck. For a model split with 20 layers on GPU and 12 on CPU, each forward pass requires 8-12 tensor transfers across PCIe per CPU-GPU boundary, each adding 0.5-2ms latency. With PCIe 3.0 x16 bandwidth of ~15GB/s, transferring a 4096-dim float16 tensor (32KB) takes 2μs, but transfer setup overhead dominates at small tensor sizes. Net result: tokens/sec can be 20-30% lower with partial offloading than pure CPU inference for small GPU layers. Optimal configuration: either all layers on GPU (n_gpu_layers=-1) or all on CPU (n_gpu_layers=0), not mixed, unless the GPU provides >50% of layers.

Using llama.cpp for batch text classification: send multiple classification requests in a single batch to llama-server using the undocumented /v1/completions with array input (supported in newer versions). For binary classification (spam/not-spam), constrain output with a grammar: grammar='root ::= "spam" | "not-spam"' to guarantee valid class labels. Temperature 0 with greedy decoding for consistency. Batch classification of 1000 emails takes ~10 minutes on RTX 3090 with Mistral-7B-Q4_K_M, versus 30+ minutes for sequential requests. Monitor throughput with /metrics endpoint. For very large batches, pipeline the requests: maintain a queue of 8 pending requests (matching --parallel 8) to keep GPU utilization near 100%.

llama.cpp maintains a separate llama-cli executable for single-prompt interactive use and a llama-server for production serving, rather than combining them into one binary. This separation allows llama-cli to be compiled with interactive features (terminal UI, readline, ANSI colors) without bloating the server binary with UI code. The server binary is designed to be daemonizable and log to files rather than stdout, with clean signal handling for graceful shutdown. For development and model testing, llama-cli provides a REPL-like experience with --interactive flag; for production, llama-server exposes a REST API. This architectural split also enables different update cadences: the server API surface changes less frequently than CLI flags.

llama.cpp's --mlock flag (lock model in RAM to prevent swapping) requires CAP_IPC_LOCK capability on Linux or administrator privileges on Windows. Without the capability, mlock() silently fails and the model can still be swapped to disk under memory pressure, causing sudden latency spikes. Symptom: inference is fast for minutes then suddenly drops to 1/10th speed during OS memory pressure events. Diagnostic: check /proc/PID/status for VmLck — it should equal VmRSS for a fully locked process. Fix on Linux: add LimitMEMLOCK=infinity to the systemd service unit, or run as root (not recommended), or use ulimit -l unlimited in the shell before starting. On cloud VMs, mlock often fails due to hypervisor restrictions on huge page allocation.

llama.cpp's context-shift mechanism (--ctx-shift flag in llama-server) enables unlimited-length conversations by automatically evicting the oldest tokens from the KV cache when the context fills up, retaining the system prompt and most recent turns. Without context shift, reaching n_ctx triggers an error and the conversation must restart. With --ctx-shift, the server performs a rolling window eviction: the first 25% of context (excluding system prompt) is dropped and the remaining context is shifted left. The model sees a truncated but coherent conversation. Quality impact: minor for simple tasks, noticeable for tasks requiring long-range context (e.g., code that references definitions from early in the conversation). Available since llama.cpp release b2963.

llama.cpp produces different output when the prompt uses the model's chat template versus raw prompt formatting. Models like Llama-3.1-Instruct have specific special tokens (<|begin_of_text|>, <|start_header_id|>system<|end_header_id|>) that must appear exactly as trained. Using llama-cli without --chat-template or using the wrong template (e.g., ChatML template for a Llama-3.1 model) causes the model to generate confused output, often repeating the system prompt or ignoring instructions. Always use llama-server's /v1/chat/completions endpoint which applies the GGUF-embedded chat template automatically, or explicitly pass --chat-template llama3 flag to llama-cli. Check template with: gguf-dump model.gguf | grep tokenizer.chat_template.

evidence_refs.excerpt: Chat template requirements for instruct models

statement: Compiling llama.cpp for optimal CPU performance on Apple Silicon: (1) git clone https://github.com/ggerganov/llama.cpp && cd llama.cpp. (2) cmake -B build -DLLAMA_METAL=ON -DLLAMA_NATIVE=ON -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release. (3) cmake --build build --config Release -j $(sysctl -n hw.logicalcpu). The -DLLAMA_METAL=ON flag enables Apple's Metal GPU API; -DLLAMA_NATIVE=ON enables ARM NEON and Apple Silicon-specific optimizations. The resulting binary automatically uses GPU for supported layers and CPU for remainder. For embedding-only workloads on Apple Silicon, the CPU path with NEON is often faster than Metal due to lower dispatch overhead for small batch sizes. Verify Metal acceleration with GGML_METAL_DEBUG=1 environment variable which logs Metal dispatch events.

evidence_refs.excerpt: Apple Silicon build instructions with Metal

statement: Implementing retrieval-augmented generation with dynamic context assembly in llama.cpp: prefill the KV cache with frequently accessed background knowledge (product documentation, FAQ) using a warm-up request at server startup. Set cache_prompt=true in subsequent requests that include this background knowledge prefix. For query-specific context, retrieve relevant chunks and append after the cached prefix. This two-tier approach separates static background knowledge (cached) from dynamic query-specific context (not cached). The cached prefix must be byte-identical across requests for cache reuse to work — even a single character difference invalidates the cache. Monitor cache hit rate with the /metrics endpoint (kv_cache_tokens_used vs total processed).

evidence_refs.excerpt: KV cache reuse with cache_prompt parameter

statement: llama.cpp's llama-quantize with the --imatrix flag corrupts quantized models when the importance matrix was generated with a different tokenizer vocabulary than the model being quantized. This happens when converting models from HuggingFace with modified tokenizer (e.g., adding domain-specific tokens post-training) and using an imatrix generated from the original model. The importance scores are stored per-token-index; if the vocabulary was modified, token indices shift and importance scores are applied to the wrong weights. Always regenerate the imatrix after any tokenizer modification. Symptom: model loads and generates text but produces significantly worse perplexity than expected for the quantization level.

llama.cpp stores GGUF model metadata as typed key-value pairs in the file header rather than a separate sidecar file, enabling self-contained model distribution. A GGUF file contains everything needed to run inference: weights, tokenizer vocabulary, special token IDs, architecture hyperparameters, and the chat template. This eliminates the HuggingFace-style requirement of having config.json, tokenizer.json, and tokenizer_config.json alongside the model weights. The trade-off is that updating metadata (e.g., fixing a chat template bug) requires rewriting the entire GGUF file or patching the header in-place with gguf-py, rather than editing a JSON config file. The self-contained design significantly simplifies deployment and model versioning.

llama.cpp's continuous batching implementation processes requests from a fixed-size slot pool (--parallel N creates N slots). When all slots are occupied by long-running requests, new requests queue and experience head-of-line blocking. Unlike transformer engines with dynamic batching (vLLM, TGI), llama.cpp does not preempt slow requests to serve fast ones. For workloads with mixed short and long requests, set --parallel to at least 2x the expected peak concurrent request count to reduce head-of-line blocking. Monitor queue depth with the /metrics endpoint queue_size metric. If queue_size frequently exceeds --parallel, consider horizontal scaling (multiple llama-server instances behind a load balancer) rather than increasing --parallel beyond GPU capacity.

evidence_refs.excerpt: Parallel request slots and queuing behavior

statement: A legal document analysis pipeline with llama.cpp: (1) PDF → text extraction with pdfminer.six, splitting into 1000-token chunks with 100-token overlap. (2) Each chunk classified as relevant/irrelevant using Mistral-7B-Q4_K_M with grammar='root ::= "relevant" | "irrelevant"'. (3) Relevant chunks assembled into a structured prompt for Mixtral-8x7B-Q4_K_M (requires 32GB RAM) for detailed analysis with JSON output grammar. (4) Results aggregated into a legal summary report. The two-model pipeline (small classifier + large analyzer) processes a 100-page contract in ~8 minutes on a Mac Studio M2 Ultra (192GB unified memory), versus 45 minutes for sequential large-model analysis of all chunks. The classifier step eliminates 60-70% of chunks as boilerplate, reducing large model compute by that proportion.

llama.cpp crashes on Linux with SIGBUS when loading large models (>10GB) on ext4 filesystems with large_file feature disabled. Old ext4 filesystems formatted with mkfs.ext4 on kernels <2.6.23 may not support files >2GB, causing mmap to fail with SIGBUS when accessing memory pages beyond the 2GB boundary. Modern systems are unlikely to encounter this, but it can occur when models are stored on old NAS devices or legacy VM disk images. Diagnostic: check filesystem features with tune2fs -l /dev/sdX | grep large_file. Fix: copy model to a modern filesystem, or re-create the filesystem with mkfs.ext4 -O large_file. This issue is distinct from the filesystem's file size limit — the limit is in the ext4 feature flags, not the partition size.

evidence_refs.excerpt: SIGBUS on old filesystems with large GGUF files

statement: Profiling llama.cpp inference to identify bottlenecks: (1) Enable profiling build: cmake -DLLAMA_PROFILING=ON .. (adds gprof instrumentation). (2) Run inference: ./llama-cli -m model.gguf -p 'test' -n 100 --verbose-prompt. (3) Analyze: gprof llama-cli gmon.out | head -50. For GPU profiling: GGML_METAL_DEBUG=1 (Metal), CUDA_LAUNCH_BLOCKING=1 (CUDA) with nvprof ./llama-server ... (4) Identify whether the bottleneck is prefill (GPU-bound, limited by FLOPS) or decode (memory-bandwidth-bound, limited by weight loading speed). Prefill-bound: reduce batch size or use speculative decoding. Decode-bound: use smaller quantization to fit more model layers in VRAM, or use speculative decoding with a small draft model.

Model hot-swapping in llama-server without downtime: llama-server does not support runtime model reloading, but blue-green deployment achieves near-zero-downtime model updates. Run two llama-server instances on ports 8080 and 8081; a nginx upstream switches between them. To update the model: (1) Start new server on 8081 with updated model and wait for it to pass /health check. (2) Update nginx upstream to point to 8081. (3) Wait for in-flight requests on 8080 to complete (monitor with /metrics). (4) Shut down 8080. The model warm-up period (first request after startup is slow due to page faults on mmap) should be handled by sending a synthetic request before updating nginx routing. Total switchover time: 30-120 seconds depending on model size and IO speed.

evidence_refs.excerpt: Server deployment and health check endpoint

statement: llama.cpp's sampler chain configuration via the --samplers flag is order-sensitive: applying temperature before top-p versus top-p before temperature produces different outputs. The default sampler chain is temperature → top-k → top-p → min-p → tfs → typical → mirostat, but users who specify --samplers 'top_p;temperature' get top-p first (restrictive) then temperature (scaling), which is mathematically different from temperature-then-top-p. With temperature=0.8 and top-p=0.9, temperature-first warms the distribution then top-p truncates; top-p-first truncates the cold distribution then temperature warms it, resulting in narrower effective sampling. Most published generation configs assume temperature-first ordering. Always verify sampler order matches the model's training configuration when replicating published results.

llama.cpp's Infill (fill-in-middle) capability extends to multi-file code context through the llama-server /infill endpoint, which accepts prefix and suffix fields that can contain code from multiple files concatenated with file boundary markers. For a code editor integration: prefix contains the current file up to cursor position plus related file imports, suffix contains the remainder of the current file. The model generates the fill-in section. DeepSeek-Coder-33B achieves 78% pass@1 on HumanEval FIM benchmarks at Q4_K_M quantization, competitive with GPT-4 Turbo at 1/1000th the cost for on-premises deployment. Integration with Language Server Protocol allows IDE plugins to invoke llama-server for inline completions.

llama.cpp's tool calling support requires a Jinja2 chat template that includes tool call formatting logic embedded in the GGUF metadata. Models without a tool-calling chat template in their GGUF (most base models and older instruct models) do not support tool calls through llama-server's /v1/chat/completions tools parameter — the server returns a 400 error or ignores the tools field. To add tool calling support to a model without it: patch the GGUF metadata to add a tool-compatible Jinja2 template using gguf-py. The Jinja2 template must correctly handle tool call generation in the model's native format (function call JSON for Llama-3.1, XML-style for Mistral). Community-maintained Jinja2 templates for popular models are available in the llama.cpp grammars directory.

evidence_refs.excerpt: Tool calling requirements and chat template

statement: Monitoring llama-server in production with Prometheus and Grafana: (1) Enable metrics: llama-server --metrics flag exposes /metrics in Prometheus exposition format. (2) Key metrics: llama_prompt_tokens_total (throughput), llama_generated_tokens_total (output rate), llama_requests_processing (queue depth), llama_kv_cache_usage_ratio (cache pressure). (3) Grafana dashboard: alert on kv_cache_usage_ratio > 0.9 (context saturation risk), requests_processing > parallel_slots × 2 (queue buildup), and generated_tokens rate drop >50% over 5 minutes (possible model hang). (4) Docker Compose example: add prometheus scrape config pointing to llama-server:8080/metrics. Open-source dashboard JSON available in the llama.cpp repository under examples/server/. This enables SLA monitoring for enterprise deployments.

llama.cpp's ARM SVE (Scalable Vector Extension) optimizations on AWS Graviton3 instances fail to compile correctly with GCC 12.x due to a compiler bug in the SVE intrinsic expansion for GCC 12.2-12.3. The symptom is incorrect matrix multiplication results causing garbled LLM output — not a crash, but semantically wrong tokens. Affected: c7g, m7g instance families with GCC 12.x toolchain (default on Amazon Linux 2023). Fix: upgrade to GCC 13+ (dnf install gcc-13) or set cmake -DLLAMA_SVE=OFF to disable SVE optimizations (falls back to NEON, ~15% slower but correct). Verify with llama-perplexity: perplexity should be within 0.1% of reference values; corrupted SVE output produces perplexity 2-10x higher than expected.

evidence_refs.excerpt: Graviton3 SVE compilation failures with GCC 12

statement: Implementing a multi-language translation service with llama.cpp using language detection + conditional routing: (1) A lightweight language detector (lingua-py or langdetect, runs in <1ms) identifies the source language. (2) Routes to the appropriate model: Llama-3.1-8B for European languages (high training data coverage), Qwen2.5-7B for CJK languages (better Chinese/Japanese/Korean representation), ALMA-13B for high-quality single-direction translation pairs. (3) All models run as separate llama-server instances, exposed through a translation API gateway. (4) Translation quality falls back to a larger model (Llama-3.1-70B) if confidence metric from the smaller model is low (measured by token probability of first output token). This cascading architecture balances cost and quality.

evidence_refs.excerpt: Multiple model server instances for routing

statement: llama.cpp's generate-vocab-only mode (--vocab-only flag) loads only the tokenizer from a GGUF file without loading model weights, enabling fast tokenization services that need to count tokens, split text, or validate token counts against context limits without the memory overhead of loading the full model. A Python wrapper around llama-cli --vocab-only can serve as a drop-in replacement for tiktoken for models where tiktoken templates are unavailable. Typical use: content pipeline that pre-tokenizes documents to estimate chunk sizes and ensure they fit within n_ctx limits. Startup time: <1 second versus 5-60 seconds for full model loading. Memory: ~50MB versus 4-40GB. This enables integrating llama.cpp tokenization into microservices where loading the full model would be prohibitive.

llama.cpp's CUDA backend hangs indefinitely on multi-GPU setups when NCCL (NVIDIA Collective Communications Library) is not installed. When --tensor-split specifies multiple GPUs, llama.cpp attempts to use NCCL for inter-GPU communication. If NCCL is not found at runtime, the CUDA backend falls back to slow PCIe transfers, but on some configurations, NCCL initialization hangs rather than failing cleanly. Symptom: server starts, loads model to GPUs, then hangs on first inference request. Fix: install libnccl-dev (sudo apt install libnccl-dev on Ubuntu) and rebuild with cmake -DLLAMA_CUDA=ON .. to link NCCL dynamically, or set NCCL_P2P_DISABLE=1 environment variable to force P2P communication off and use fallback paths. For single-GPU operation, the issue does not arise.

llama.cpp implements its own tensor computation library (GGML) rather than depending on PyTorch or TensorFlow because PyTorch's C++ API (libtorch) adds 500MB+ of binary dependencies and requires a Python runtime, undermining llama.cpp's zero-dependency goal. GGML is purpose-built for CPU/GPU inference of transformer architectures with a narrow feature set: tensor operations needed for attention and FFN layers, quantized matrix multiplication, and device memory management. By controlling the computation library, llama.cpp can implement quantization-specific optimizations (SIMD for Q4_K_M multiplication) that general-purpose frameworks cannot provide without a plugin system. GGML's codebase is ~50K lines versus PyTorch's 3M+ lines, making it auditable and embeddable in security-sensitive contexts.

llama.cpp's llama-server fails to start on macOS when another process holds the port, but the error message 'Address already in use' is suppressed if the server crashes during Metal initialization (which happens before socket binding). The symptom is: server process exits within 2 seconds with exit code 1 but no visible error output. Diagnostic: run llama-server with GGML_METAL_LOG_LEVEL=5 environment variable to enable verbose Metal logging, which reveals the actual failure reason. Common Metal initialization failures: Xcode Command Line Tools not installed (brew xcode-select --install), Metal framework missing on non-Apple systems (should never happen on genuine macOS), or model file permission errors before Metal allocation. On macOS 14+, Metal initialization is deferred — this changes the error timing.

llama.cpp's SYCL backend (for Intel GPUs via oneAPI) requires the Intel oneAPI Base Toolkit to be installed and the SYCL environment to be sourced before running: source /opt/intel/oneapi/setvars.sh. Without sourcing this file, the binary either fails to start or silently runs on CPU despite being compiled with SYCL support. The oneAPI environment sets SYCL_DEVICE_FILTER and LD_LIBRARY_PATH to point to Intel's SYCL runtime libraries. For Docker deployments, the oneAPI libraries must be copied into the container or the oneAPI container base image must be used. Intel Arc GPUs (Battlemage, Alchemist) benefit most from SYCL backend, achieving 20-30 tokens/sec for 7B Q4 models — competitive with AMD Radeon via ROCm for similar price points.

Using llama.cpp for automated test data generation: (1) llama-server runs with a grammar that enforces valid JSON matching the test schema. (2) A Python driver sends prompts like 'Generate a diverse set of edge-case inputs for function f(x: int, y: str) that tests boundary conditions, null inputs, and unicode edge cases'. (3) Grammar forces valid JSON array output with typed fields. (4) Generated test cases are deduped and fed to pytest. This pattern generates 50-100 diverse test cases per minute on a 7B model. Quality evaluation: measure test case diversity with embedding cosine similarity (low average similarity = high diversity) and mutation testing coverage. Models with strong code understanding (DeepSeek-Coder, Codestral) produce higher-quality edge cases than general instruction models.

llama.cpp build fails on Ubuntu 20.04 with the error 'error: 'format' is not a member of 'std'' when compiling with C++17. The root cause is that Ubuntu 20.04 ships GCC 9.3 which has incomplete C++20/C++17 std::format support. llama.cpp requires C++17 minimum and optionally uses C++20 features when detected. Fix: install GCC 12+ (sudo add-apt-repository ppa:ubuntu-toolchain-r/test && sudo apt install gcc-12 g++-12) and set cmake -DCMAKE_CXX_COMPILER=g++-12 .. alternatively use clang 14+ which has full C++17 support on 20.04. Or use the provided Docker image which is based on Ubuntu 22.04 with GCC 12. Ubuntu 22.04 and later have GCC 11+ by default and do not have this issue.

evidence_refs.excerpt: Linux build requirements and compiler versions

statement: Implementing a local coding assistant (Cursor-like) with llama.cpp: (1) llama-server runs DeepSeek-Coder-V2-Lite-Q4_K_M on --n-gpu-layers 99 --ctx-size 32768 --parallel 2. (2) A VS Code extension captures the active file, cursor position, and 5 surrounding files as context. (3) For inline completion: POST /infill with {prefix: code_before_cursor, suffix: code_after_cursor} and stream=true. (4) For chat: POST /v1/chat/completions with the full file as context. (5) Latency optimization: maintain a WebSocket connection to llama-server to avoid TCP handshake overhead for each completion. (6) Debounce trigger: 500ms after last keystroke to avoid excessive requests. The entire pipeline runs locally, ensuring code privacy. First token latency: ~200ms on RTX 4090, acceptable for IDE use.

evidence_refs.excerpt: FIM for IDE code completion integration

statement: llama.cpp's token healing feature (experimental, --token-healing flag) re-processes the last N tokens of the prompt to fix tokenization artifacts caused by prompt truncation at non-token boundaries. Without healing, appending incomplete words at the end of a prompt can force the tokenizer to produce a suboptimal token sequence for the following generation, reducing coherence. With token healing enabled, the server backtracks and re-tokenizes the boundary region, producing a more natural transition from prompt to generation. Most beneficial for: code completion where variable names are split, template-based prompting where fill-in positions land at token boundaries, and streaming completions where partial output is fed back as subsequent prompts.

evidence_refs.excerpt: Token healing for tokenization boundary artifacts

statement: llama.cpp's Flash Attention implementation (--flash-attn flag, reduces KV cache memory by not materializing the full N×N attention matrix) is incompatible with certain older quantization formats (Q5_0, Q5_1) and produces incorrect attention scores when enabled with these formats. The symptom is repetitive or incoherent output, not a crash. Always verify compatibility: check the GGUF quantization type with gguf-dump model.gguf | grep general.quantization_version before enabling Flash Attention. Flash Attention is compatible with Q4_K_M, Q8_0, F16, and F32 formats. The performance benefit is significant for long contexts: Flash Attention reduces KV cache GPU memory by O(N) compared to O(N²) standard attention, enabling 8K+ context on 8GB GPUs where standard attention would OOM.

evidence_refs.excerpt: Flash Attention compatibility and memory benefits

statement: Zero-downtime model version upgrade with llama.cpp using canary deployment: (1) New model validated offline with llama-perplexity (perplexity within 5% of previous version). (2) llama-server new version launched on port 8081 alongside existing 8080. (3) Nginx routes 10% of traffic to 8081 using upstream weight configuration. (4) Monitor error rates and latency for 30 minutes using /metrics endpoint. (5) If metrics pass: route 100% to 8081, wait for 8080 in-flight requests to drain (check requests_processing metric = 0). (6) Shut down 8080. Rollback: change nginx weight back to 100% on 8080 in under 5 seconds. This procedure requires no application downtime and limits blast radius of a bad model update to 10% of traffic during validation.

llama.cpp's --override-kv flag for modifying GGUF metadata at runtime only affects a limited set of architectural parameters — it does not allow overriding tokenizer vocabulary, attention head count, or layer count. Attempting to override architecture parameters that change tensor shapes (e.g., llama.override_kv='llama.attention.head_count=32' for a model with 64 heads) causes a shape mismatch during weight loading and immediate crash. Safe override targets: context extension parameters (rope.freq_base, rope.scaling.*), quantization hints, and chat template string. Always test overrides on a copy of the GGUF before deploying to production, as invalid overrides cause unrecoverable startup failures.

Implementing semantic similarity search using llama.cpp as the embedding backend and FAISS for vector indexing: (1) llama-server --embedding --pooling mean started with nomic-embed-text-v1.5 GGUF model. (2) Document ingestion: POST /v1/embeddings with batches of 50 text chunks, collect float32 vectors. (3) Build FAISS index: faiss.IndexFlatIP (inner product = cosine similarity for normalized vectors) or IndexIVFPQ for >1M documents. (4) Query: embed question, faiss.search(query_vector, k=5) returns top-5 chunk IDs. (5) Retrieve text from storage, pass to llama-server /v1/chat/completions for generation. Key: normalize embeddings before indexing (divide by L2 norm) to enable cosine similarity via inner product. This pipeline handles millions of documents on a single server with sub-100ms retrieval latency.

evidence_refs.excerpt: Embedding mode for semantic search pipelines

statement: llama.cpp uses a push-based compute graph (GGML computation graph) rather than an eager execution model, allowing the entire forward pass to be planned and optimized before execution. The computation graph is constructed once during model loading, then executed repeatedly with different input data. This enables: (1) graph-level optimization passes (constant folding, operation fusion), (2) memory planning that reuses buffers for intermediate tensors, reducing peak memory 30-40%, (3) GPU kernel scheduling that minimizes CPU-GPU synchronization points. The push-based model is the reason llama.cpp achieves high GPU utilization despite being single-threaded on the CPU side — the GPU executes the pre-planned graph autonomously while CPU prepares the next request.

evidence_refs.excerpt: GGML computation graph design and optimization

statement: llama.cpp's llama-batched-bench example crashes with 'std::bad_alloc' when --n-sequences exceeds available RAM because it allocates separate output buffers for each sequence. Unlike llama-server's streaming mode, llama-batched-bench materializes all outputs in memory simultaneously. For benchmarking 1000 sequences × 512 output tokens × 2 bytes/token = 1GB peak allocation — manageable. But llama-batched-bench with n_sequences=10000 requires 10GB+ RAM for output storage alone. Fix: reduce --n-sequences or run benchmarks in chunks. For production throughput benchmarking, use llama-server's /metrics endpoint instead, which measures real request throughput without materializing all outputs.

llama.cpp supports Mixture of Experts (MoE) models including Mixtral-8x7B, Mixtral-8x22B, and DeepSeek-V2 through native architecture support in GGML. MoE models activate only a subset of expert FFN layers per token (2 of 8 for Mixtral), making them faster than dense models of equivalent total parameter count. llama.cpp offloads MoE layers to GPU with expert-specific quantization — each expert can be quantized independently, with frequently-used experts kept at higher precision. On a single RTX 3090 (24GB), Mixtral-8x7B at Q4_K_M (26GB) can be partially GPU-offloaded (70% of layers) achieving ~15 tokens/sec, versus ~2 tokens/sec pure CPU — a 7x speedup for partial offloading that is atypically good because MoE sparsity reduces per-token VRAM bandwidth.

evidence_refs.excerpt: MoE model support and performance characteristics

statement: llama.cpp's llama-parallel example for benchmarking multiple simultaneous inference streams is not suitable for production serving — it is a single-binary benchmark tool that terminates after completing all prompts, not a persistent server. Production serving requires llama-server with --parallel flag. The --parallel flag on llama-server creates N independent KV cache slots sharing one model weight copy, whereas llama-parallel creates N separate model instances (N × model memory). For memory-efficient multi-tenant serving, llama-server --parallel is always preferred over running N llama-cli processes. Each llama-server slot adds O(n_ctx × n_layers) memory for its KV cache but zero additional weight memory.

evidence_refs.excerpt: Parallel example vs. server parallel flag

statement: Validating a newly quantized GGUF model before production deployment: (1) Basic sanity: ./llama-cli -m model.gguf -p 'What is 2+2?' -n 20 should output '4'. (2) Perplexity check: ./llama-perplexity -m model.gguf -f wiki.test.raw --chunks 5 should produce perplexity within 5% of published reference. (3) Format check: gguf-dump model.gguf | grep -E 'tokenizer|architecture|rope' to verify metadata. (4) Throughput baseline: ./llama-bench -m model.gguf -p 512 -n 128 to establish tokens/sec reference. (5) Grammar validation: test with a JSON grammar to verify structured output works. (6) Tool calling test (if applicable): send a tools request to llama-server and verify tool call JSON is generated correctly. This 6-step validation catches 95% of quantization artifacts before they reach users.

llama.cpp's automatic model download via --hf-repo flag fails on systems with restrictive SSL certificate policies (corporate proxies with SSL inspection) because llama.cpp uses libcurl with the system certificate store, which may not trust the proxy's root CA. Symptom: SSL certificate verification error when downloading from huggingface.co. Fix: set CURL_CA_BUNDLE=/etc/ssl/certs/ca-certificates.crt to the proxy's CA bundle, or set LLAMA_CURL_SSL_VERIFY=0 (insecure, not recommended for production). Best practice for corporate environments: download models to a local model registry (MinIO, Artifactory) and use file:// paths or a local HTTP server instead of downloading directly from HuggingFace at inference server startup.

Using llama.cpp for automated documentation generation: (1) Parse source code with tree-sitter to extract function signatures, docstrings, and call graphs. (2) For each undocumented function, construct a prompt with function body + 3 example calls + project coding style samples. (3) Grammar-constrained generation ensures output matches docstring format (Google Style, NumPy Style, JSDoc). (4) Post-process to validate generated docstrings with doctest (Python) or jest (TypeScript). (5) Auto-commit documentation PRs for review. A 100K-line codebase can be documented in 4-6 hours on a single 34B model instance. Quality benchmarks: LLM-generated docs rated 'useful' by developers 78% of the time versus 95% for human-written, with 10x the documentation coverage achievable per engineer-hour.

llama.cpp's streaming endpoint returns chunked HTTP responses, and some reverse proxies (nginx with default config, Apache with mod_proxy) buffer the response body before forwarding to the client, defeating the streaming purpose. Client receives all tokens at once after generation completes rather than token-by-token. Fix for nginx: proxy_buffering off; proxy_cache off; X-Accel-Buffering: no header in the upstream response. For Apache: ProxyPassReverse with flushpackets=on. For AWS ALB: increase idle timeout to exceed expected generation time (default 60s is too short for long generations). This is the most common production streaming problem reported in llama.cpp issues — always configure proxy buffering before testing streaming in any load-balanced setup.

llama.cpp implements Mamba (state space model) architecture support in addition to transformer architectures, enabling inference of Mamba-based models (Mamba-2.8B, Falcon Mamba 7B) that offer O(1) per-token inference time regardless of context length. Unlike transformers where KV cache grows linearly with context, Mamba maintains a fixed-size recurrent state. This makes Mamba models ideal for streaming long documents where transformer models would OOM. However, Mamba is not parallelizable during decode — it must process tokens sequentially, making it slower per token than transformers on GPU for short sequences but faster for very long contexts (>32K tokens). llama.cpp's Mamba support uses the same GGUF format and REST API as transformer models.

llama.cpp's Python bindings (llama-cpp-python) are a thin wrapper around the C++ library with a separate release cycle and installation complexity: it compiles the C++ code during pip install, requiring a working C++ compiler, cmake, and (optionally) CUDA toolkit. Installation frequently fails on systems where these build tools are missing or version-mismatched. The prebuilt wheels available on PyPI cover only common platform combinations (linux/x86_64/CUDA12.x, macOS/arm64). For custom configurations (ROCm, Vulkan, specific CUDA versions), users must build from source with pip install llama-cpp-python --config-settings cmake.args='-DLLAMA_CUDA=ON'. The Python bindings add ~50ms overhead per inference call due to ctypes FFI marshaling, which is negligible for normal use but significant for high-frequency embedding calls.

Using llama.cpp for knowledge graph construction from unstructured text: (1) llama-server runs a model capable of structured extraction with JSON grammar. (2) For each document chunk, send a prompt requesting extraction of entities and relationships in the schema {entities: [{name, type, attributes}], relationships: [{source, target, type, confidence}]}. (3) Grammar enforces the JSON output format. (4) Extracted triples are loaded into a Neo4j or Kuzu graph database. (5) Multi-hop queries traverse the knowledge graph to answer questions requiring inference across multiple documents. This pipeline processes 1000 scientific papers in ~2 hours on a 13B model, extracting 50,000-100,000 entities. Precision/recall for entity extraction: ~70% for well-defined domains (medical, legal) versus ~50% for open domains.

llama.cpp's --dump-kv-cache debug flag causes significant performance degradation (10-20x slower) when enabled in production because it serializes the full KV cache to disk on every generation step. This flag is intended for debugging KV cache behavior, not for production monitoring. Symptom of accidentally enabling it: llama-server generates tokens at <1 token/sec despite normal GPU utilization.
