Check for this flag in service configuration files, especially after copying configuration from a debugging session to production. The correct monitoring approach is the /metrics Prometheus endpoint, which adds <1ms overhead per request.

evidence_refs.excerpt: Debug flags that impact production performance

statement: llama.cpp supports Retrieval-Augmented Generation with citation tracking through structured output: request JSON output with format {answer: string, citations: [{chunk_id: string, text: string, relevance_score: float}]} using a grammar constraint. The model identifies which retrieved chunks it used to generate each part of the answer. This enables attribution and fact-checking workflows where users can verify claims against source documents. For highest-quality citation tracking, include chunk IDs in the retrieved context passed to the model: [CHUNK-001] <text> [CHUNK-002] <text> and instruct the model to reference chunk IDs in citations. Models trained on academic papers (Llama-3.1-based research variants) produce more accurate citations than general instruction models.

llama.cpp fails to correctly handle models with GQA (Grouped Query Attention) when --n-gpu-layers splits GQA and non-GQA layers across CPU and GPU. In GQA models (Llama-3, Mistral), key-value heads are fewer than query heads (e.g., 8 KV heads, 32 query heads). When the CPU handles the attention computation for layers not offloaded to GPU, the CPU GGML kernels must correctly broadcast the 8 KV heads to 32 query heads. A bug in older llama.cpp versions (pre-b2800) caused incorrect broadcasting on CPU GQA layers, producing garbled output for CPU-GPU split inference. Fix: update llama.cpp to the latest release; GQA is fully supported on GPU. For models with GQA, prefer --n-gpu-layers 99 to keep all attention on GPU.

llama.cpp releases binaries as GitHub Actions artifacts rather than through a package manager (apt, brew, pip) because the diversity of hardware configurations (GPU backends, ISA extensions, OS versions) makes it impractical to maintain a universal pre-built binary. A binary with CUDA 12.4 support would not work on CUDA 11.8 systems; a binary with AVX512 optimizations would crash on pre-Skylake CPUs. The artifact-per-configuration approach lets users download exactly the binary matching their environment. The trade-off is discovery friction: users must identify their configuration and navigate GitHub releases. For automated deployment, the CMake build system with configuration flags is the recommended approach, while pre-built binaries are for manual testing and evaluation.

Setting up llama.cpp for multi-modal document understanding (images + text): (1) Download a LLaVA-compatible model: llava-llama-3-8b or BakLLaVA-1 as two GGUF files (base + mmproj). (2) Start: llama-server -m llava-base.gguf --mmproj llava-clip.gguf --n-gpu-layers 99 --ctx-size 8192. (3) Test with image: curl -X POST /v1/chat/completions -d '{messages:[{role:"user",content:[{type:"image_url",image_url:{url:"data:image/jpeg;base64,BASE64"}},{type:"text",text:"Describe this image"}]}]}'. (4) For document processing: convert PDF pages to JPEG (pdf2image library), send each page as image with specific extraction prompt. LLaVA achieves ~85% accuracy on document OCR tasks versus ~95% for dedicated OCR tools, but enables semantic understanding beyond character recognition.

evidence_refs.excerpt: LLaVA multi-modal setup and document processing

statement: llama.cpp's RoPE scaling for context extension to 128K+ tokens (Llama-3.1 style) requires the model to have been specifically trained or fine-tuned with extended context RoPE. Simply setting --rope-freq-base 500000 on a model trained with 4K context enables the model to accept longer inputs without crashing, but quality degrades catastrophically beyond the original training context length — hallucinations increase and coherence decreases. Quality degrades gradually starting at 1.5× the training context (6K for a 4K model) and becomes unusable at 3× (12K). Genuine long-context capability requires the Llama-3.1 variant specifically fine-tuned with 128K context. Always check GGUF metadata gguf-dump | grep context_length for the actual supported context length.

evidence_refs.excerpt: RoPE context extension requirements and limitations

statement: llama.cpp's batch API via llama-server hangs when a single request in a batch uses an extremely long context that fills the KV cache, blocking all parallel slots. With --parallel 4 and --ctx-size 4096 (total 16384 KV slots), a single request with 15000-token context consumes 92% of all KV slots, leaving insufficient capacity for the other 3 parallel slots. New requests queue until the long request completes. This head-of-line blocking is worse than sequential serving in high-context workloads. Mitigation: set per-request context limits at the application layer before sending to llama-server; reject requests longer than (n_ctx × 0.75 / n_parallel) tokens. Monitor kv_cache_usage_ratio metric — values above 0.9 indicate imminent blocking.

evidence_refs.excerpt: KV cache slot allocation with parallel requests

statement: Implementing a Q&A evaluation harness with llama.cpp: (1) llama-server serves the model under evaluation. (2) A Python driver reads benchmark datasets (MMLU, HellaSwag, TruthfulQA) in JSONL format. (3) For each question, sends multiple-choice prompt with grammar='root ::= "A" | "B" | "C" | "D"' to force single-letter answers. (4) Compares generated answer against ground truth, computes accuracy per category. (5) Results stored in SQLite with model name, quantization type, and timestamp for longitudinal tracking. (6) Statistical significance testing (bootstrapped confidence intervals) determines if quantization reduces accuracy significantly. This evaluation pipeline runs MMLU (14K questions) in ~4 hours on a 7B model, providing evidence-based quantization quality certification.

llama.cpp's /v1/chat/completions endpoint with stream=false returns the full response in one HTTP response body, but clients with short read timeouts (common default: 30s) time out before the response completes for long generations. A 1000-token generation at 10 tokens/sec takes 100 seconds, well beyond most HTTP client defaults. The proper fix is not to increase timeout — it is to use stream=true with SSE and process tokens incrementally, resetting the timeout on each received chunk. For batch processing where streaming UX is not needed, increase the HTTP client timeout to max_tokens / expected_tokens_per_sec × 2 as a safety margin. Always test with the longest expected generation length, not average length.

evidence_refs.excerpt: Streaming vs. blocking response and timeout considerations

statement: llama.cpp's llama-export-lora binary merges trained LoRA adapters back into the base model GGUF, producing a standalone GGUF without adapter overhead. A 7B model with a rank-64 LoRA adapter runs 15-20% slower than the base model due to the extra matrix multiplications for the adapter computation (W + αBA). After merging with llama-export-lora, inference speed returns to baseline. The merge also enables quantizing the merged model — you can fine-tune on F16, then merge and quantize to Q4_K_M, achieving both fine-tuning customization and quantization efficiency. Workflow: train LoRA → merge → quantize → deploy, treating the merged-quantized model as the final artifact.

evidence_refs.excerpt: LoRA adapter merging and quantization workflow

statement: llama.cpp's grammar-constrained generation increases time-to-first-token because the GBNF grammar must be compiled to an internal representation at request time, not at server startup. A complex grammar (full JSON Schema with 20+ properties) can add 50-200ms per request for grammar compilation. For high-frequency requests with the same grammar, cache the compiled grammar by passing the same grammar string — the server caches recent grammars by hash. For a shared system prompt + grammar across all requests, move the grammar to a server-level default rather than per-request. Grammar compilation cost scales with grammar complexity: simple grammars (enum of 5 values) add <1ms, while complex nested object schemas add 100ms+.

Integrating llama.cpp with LangChain or LlamaIndex for RAG pipelines: use the OpenAI-compatible endpoint at http://localhost:8080/v1 with a custom base_url. For LangChain: from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI; llm = ChatOpenAI(base_url='http://localhost:8080/v1', api_key='your-key', model='llama-3.1-8b'). For LlamaIndex: from llama_index.llms.openai import OpenAI; llm = OpenAI(base_url='http://localhost:8080/v1', api_key='key'). Both frameworks will use llama-server as if it were OpenAI's API. Limitations: function calling works only if the model's GGUF includes a tool-compatible chat template; streaming works; embeddings require a separate embedding model instance. This integration enables using the full LangChain/LlamaIndex ecosystem with locally-running models.

evidence_refs.excerpt: OpenAI API compatibility for framework integration

statement: llama.cpp's CUDA backend produces NaN (not-a-number) values during inference when using FP16 half-precision accumulation on Volta-generation GPUs (V100) with large context lengths. The issue is that FP16 addition can overflow for very large attention scores that arise in long sequences, producing inf which propagates as NaN. Turing and later generations (RTX 20xx+) handle this better due to improved FP16 saturation semantics. Fix for V100: compile with -DLLAMA_CUDA_F16=OFF to use FP32 accumulation in attention (20-30% slower but numerically stable). Symptom: generations work correctly for short prompts (<2K tokens) but produce repetitive garbage for longer prompts. Check: grep NaN in output or look for token logit debug output showing NaN values.

evidence_refs.excerpt: FP16 overflow on V100 with long contexts

statement: Autonomous research assistant with llama.cpp tool use: (1) llama-server serves Llama-3.1-70B-Q4_K_M with tool calling enabled via Jinja2 chat template. (2) Python orchestrator defines tools: web_search(query), read_url(url), save_note(content), generate_report(notes). (3) Agentic loop: send task to model with tools, receive tool call JSON, execute the Python tool function, send result back to model, repeat until model calls generate_report. (4) Grammar enforces valid tool call JSON format. (5) Budget guard: max 20 tool calls per task to prevent runaway loops. The 70B model reliably uses tools for 2-3 hop reasoning tasks; 8B models succeed on single-tool calls but struggle with multi-step planning. Entire pipeline runs locally with no API costs.

llama.cpp's OpenCL backend (legacy, now superseded by Vulkan) is deprecated and not maintained for new architectures. Attempting to build with -DLLAMA_OPENCL=ON on systems with modern GPUs (RDNA2+, Ada Lovelace) produces compilation warnings about unsupported features and may crash or produce incorrect results at runtime. The OpenCL backend was frozen before GQA, MoE, and Flash Attention support, meaning OpenCL builds will fail or produce wrong output for any model using these features. Users with OpenCL-only GPUs should build with -DLLAMA_VULKAN=ON instead, which has active maintenance and full feature support. The last release with maintained OpenCL support was llama.cpp b2000 (approximately late 2023).

Continuous learning pipeline with llama.cpp for personal knowledge management: (1) llama-server generates daily summaries of new notes, articles, and conversations stored in a local vault. (2) llama-embedding generates embeddings for all new content. (3) A similarity search identifies related existing notes to link. (4) llama-server generates a 'connection insights' report identifying non-obvious relationships between recent and older content. (5) Weekly, a consolidation pass identifies duplicate knowledge and suggests merges. This pipeline runs as a scheduled cron job (midnight), taking 10-20 minutes per day on a 7B model for a vault of 5000+ notes. The result is a dynamic knowledge graph that surfaces non-obvious connections without manual tagging.

llama.cpp prioritizes the GGUF file format over HuggingFace safetensors because GGUF was designed to address safetensors' limitations for inference: safetensors only stores raw float32/float16/bfloat16 tensors with no quantization support, no architecture metadata, and no tokenizer information. For inference use cases, the GGUF self-contained format (weights + metadata + tokenizer in one file) enables standalone deployment without requiring a Python environment to load config.json or tokenizer.json. Safetensors remains better for training (PyTorch compatibility, gradient checkpointing) while GGUF is better for inference (quantization, portability). The converter scripts in llama.cpp bridge the two formats for the training-then-deploy workflow.

llama.cpp's llama-server does not support model switching at runtime — each server instance is bound to one model loaded at startup. Multi-model serving requires either: (1) Multiple server instances on different ports with a routing layer, (2) Model loading/unloading via a custom wrapper that terminates and restarts the server process, or (3) Using a higher-level orchestration tool (Ollama, LM Studio) that handles model lifecycle. Approach (2) has 5-60 second model swap latency depending on model size. For applications requiring <1 second model switch time, the only option is pre-loading all needed models in separate server processes (high memory cost) or using a purpose-built multi-model server. This limitation is by design: single-model optimization is the primary focus of llama.cpp.

evidence_refs.excerpt: Single model per server instance design

statement: Debugging output quality issues with llama.cpp (garbled/incoherent output): Step 1: Rule out quantization — try F16 model variant; if output improves, lower quantization (Q2_K, Q3_K) is the cause. Step 2: Rule out chat template — try raw completion without system prompt; if coherent, the chat template is wrong for the model. Step 3: Rule out context overflow — reduce --ctx-size to 1024; if output improves, context truncation was corrupting state. Step 4: Rule out sampling — set temperature 0 and top-p 1.0; if consistent output, sampling parameters are causing variance. Step 5: Verify model integrity — gguf-dump model.gguf | grep md5 to check checksums if available. Step 6: Test with official llama.cpp examples using their hardcoded prompts as a sanity check. This systematic elimination approach resolves 90% of quality issues without requiring llama.cpp source code knowledge.

evidence_refs.excerpt: Debugging guide for inference quality issues

statement: llama.cpp's seed parameter for reproducibility does not guarantee identical output when the context window contains timestamps, random-seeded text, or other non-deterministic prompt elements. The seed controls the sampling RNG, not the model's internal state, so if two requests have identical seeds but different prompts (e.g., the prompt includes the current date), the outputs will differ. For true reproducibility in testing: (1) Fix the prompt completely (no dynamic elements), (2) Set --seed with a fixed value, (3) Set temperature 0 (greedy), (4) Set --parallel 1. Even then, different GPU driver versions may produce subtly different floating-point results, breaking seed-based reproducibility at the bit level while being semantically identical. Consider semantic comparison rather than byte-level comparison for test assertions.

llama.cpp supports Phi-3 and Phi-3.5 architecture family models with full tool calling and vision capabilities through native architecture support added in llama.cpp b3000+. Phi-3-mini (3.8B) runs at 50+ tokens/sec on RTX 3090 at Q4_K_M, making it the fastest instruction model available in the GGUF ecosystem for tasks requiring <4K context. Phi-3.5-MoE (16x3.8B) provides Mistral-quality outputs at 2x the speed due to MoE sparsity. For edge deployment (Raspberry Pi 5 with 8GB RAM), Phi-3-mini Q4_K_M (2.2GB) is the recommended model, achieving 3-5 tokens/sec on ARM Cortex-A76 — sufficient for interactive local AI assistant applications.

A home automation natural language interface with llama.cpp: (1) llama-server runs Phi-3-mini Q4_K_M on a Raspberry Pi 5 (3-5 tok/s sufficient for command parsing). (2) Voice input via whisper.cpp on the same Pi. (3) Recognized speech sent to llama-server with a tool calling prompt defining functions: set_light(room, brightness, color), set_thermostat(temperature), query_sensor(sensor_id). (4) JSON tool call output is parsed and executed via Home Assistant REST API. (5) llama-server returns a natural language confirmation. Entire pipeline runs locally — no cloud, no privacy concerns. Voice-to-action latency: 2-4 seconds (1s transcription + 2-3s LLM). Grammar constraint on tool call output ensures 100% parseable JSON even on this small model.

llama.cpp's llama-server /v1/completions (legacy completions API) and /v1/chat/completions produce different outputs for the same underlying content due to different prompt formatting paths. /v1/completions passes the prompt directly to the model with no template processing; /v1/chat/completions applies the Jinja2 chat template. For instruct models, always use /v1/chat/completions — using /v1/completions with an instruct model produces poor results because the model expects system/user/assistant tags that are absent. Conversely, using /v1/chat/completions with a base model adds template tokens the model wasn't trained with, potentially confusing it. Match endpoint to model type: chat completions for instruct models, completions for base models.

evidence_refs.excerpt: Chat completions vs completions endpoint routing

statement: llama.cpp's maximum supported vocabulary size is limited by the GGUF token embedding table dimension, which must fit in GPU memory as a contiguous block. For models with very large vocabularies (Qwen-2.5 has 152,064 tokens; LLaMA-3 has 128,256 tokens), the token embedding table alone occupies 152,064 × embedding_dim × bytes_per_element — for Qwen-2.5-72B this is 152,064 × 8192 × 2 bytes = 2.5GB just for embeddings. On GPU, this embedding table must be loaded fully even with quantization because token lookups require random access. This constrains which models fit on smaller GPUs: a model with 152K vocabulary needs ~2.5GB more VRAM than an equivalent architecture with 32K vocabulary, regardless of model depth or quantization.

evidence_refs.excerpt: Vocabulary size impact on GPU memory

statement: Cross-compiling llama.cpp for ARM Linux (Raspberry Pi, Jetson Nano) from an x86 host: (1) Install cross-compiler: sudo apt install gcc-aarch64-linux-gnu g++-aarch64-linux-gnu. (2) Create CMake toolchain file: set(CMAKE_SYSTEM_NAME Linux), set(CMAKE_SYSTEM_PROCESSOR aarch64), set(CMAKE_C_COMPILER aarch64-linux-gnu-gcc), set(CMAKE_CXX_COMPILER aarch64-linux-gnu-g++). (3) cmake -B build-arm -DCMAKE_TOOLCHAIN_FILE=toolchain-aarch64.cmake -DLLAMA_NATIVE=OFF (must disable for cross-compile). (4) cmake --build build-arm -j8. For Jetson (CUDA ARM): use NVIDIA's jetson-containers Docker image which provides the correct CUDA ARM toolchain. Key: -DLLAMA_NATIVE=OFF is mandatory for cross-compilation to prevent host-specific SIMD flags being applied to target binary.

Implementing confidence estimation for llama.cpp outputs using token log-probabilities: set logprobs=true in /v1/completions requests to receive the log-probability of each generated token. A high-confidence generation has most tokens with logprob > -0.5 (probability > 60%); a low-confidence generation has many tokens with logprob < -2.0 (probability < 14%). Aggregate metric: mean logprob of all generated tokens as an output confidence score. Use this to route low-confidence outputs to human review or to a larger model for validation. Calibration: measure actual accuracy versus predicted confidence on a held-out dataset to verify the logprob signal correlates with correctness for your specific task. This pattern is particularly valuable for information extraction where wrong but confident outputs are more harmful than abstentions.

llama.cpp's model loading fails with 'tensor 'token_embd.weight' not found' when loading a GGUF file that was converted from a model using architecture-specific tensor naming different from llama.cpp's expected schema. This happens with: (1) Custom model architectures not officially supported in convert_hf_to_gguf.py, (2) Models converted with a very old llama.cpp converter before architecture detection was added, (3) GGUF files from non-llama.cpp sources that use different tensor naming conventions. Diagnosis: gguf-dump model.gguf | grep -E 'blk\.0\.attn|token_embd|output\.weight' to see actual tensor names versus expected names in llama.cpp source (llama.cpp file, ggml_backend_tensor_get). The fix requires patching the converter to map source names to llama.cpp-expected names.

evidence_refs.excerpt: Tensor naming requirements for GGUF compatibility

statement: llama.cpp's decision to use a REST API for llama-server (rather than gRPC or a custom binary protocol) reflects a deliberate trade-off: HTTP/REST has higher per-request overhead (~1-2ms versus ~0.1ms for gRPC) but is universally supported by every HTTP client in every language without code generation or protobuf dependencies. For LLM inference where generation takes 1-100 seconds, the 1-2ms REST overhead is negligible (<0.1%). gRPC would provide advantages only for very high-frequency short requests (tokenization, classification with <10ms generation time), which are edge cases in LLM serving. The REST choice also enables browser-native clients via fetch API and easy integration with existing API infrastructure.

llama.cpp supports RWKV (Receptance Weighted Key Value) architecture models, which are recurrent neural networks that avoid the quadratic attention complexity of transformers. RWKV models run at O(1) memory for any context length because they maintain a fixed-size recurrent state rather than a growing KV cache. For extremely long document processing (>100K tokens), RWKV models at 7B parameters can run on 8GB VRAM where a transformer model would require 8× more VRAM for the KV cache. Trade-off: RWKV quality is generally below equivalent-size transformer models for complex reasoning tasks, but competitive for summarization and question-answering over long documents. Convert RWKV models with the dedicated convert_rwkv_to_gguf.py script.

llama.cpp cannot quantize models directly from HuggingFace format — conversion to GGUF F16 must happen first. A common mistake is trying to run ./llama-quantize directly on a .safetensors model or a directory of .bin files; the binary rejects non-GGUF inputs with a format mismatch error. The two-step workflow is mandatory: (1) python convert_hf_to_gguf.py /model_dir --outtype f16 to produce the F16 GGUF, (2) ./llama-quantize model-f16.gguf model-q4.gguf Q4_K_M to quantize. The conversion step can be memory-intensive for large models (70B F16 = 140GB RAM required for full model in memory during conversion). For memory-constrained systems, use the --no-model-load-on-convert flag if available, or convert on a cloud instance with sufficient RAM.

Setting up llama.cpp with Kubernetes for scalable inference: (1) Build Docker image: FROM nvidia/cuda:12.4.1-runtime-ubuntu22.04 with the llama-server binary compiled with CUDA support. (2) Kubernetes Deployment with resources.limits.nvidia.com/gpu: 1, --parallel 4 per pod. (3) Horizontal Pod Autoscaler based on custom metric llama_requests_processing (from Prometheus). (4) Shared ReadWriteMany NFS volume for model files (avoids re-downloading per pod), with model pre-cached to prevent slow first-request. (5) Service with sessionAffinity: ClientIP to ensure requests from same user go to same pod (for KV cache reuse). (6) Liveness probe: GET /health with 30s timeout. This architecture scales from 1 to N GPUs automatically based on queue depth, handling variable load without over-provisioning.

llama.cpp's batch embedding endpoint (/v1/embeddings with array input) silently truncates inputs that exceed n_ctx tokens without returning an error. The truncated embedding is computed from only the first n_ctx tokens, producing a lower-quality representation of the full document without any warning. This is particularly problematic for RAG pipelines that send long document chunks for embedding — the chunks may have been sized for a different embedding model with larger context. Detection: compare embedding norms for normal versus oversized inputs; truncated embeddings often have similar norms, making the issue invisible without explicit token-count validation. Prevention: tokenize all texts before embedding and filter/chunk texts that exceed n_ctx × 0.9 as a safety margin.

Implementing a local AI writing assistant with style transfer using llama.cpp: (1) llama-server runs a 13B instruction model. (2) System prompt encodes the target writing style extracted from reference documents (e.g., 3000 tokens of the user's previous writing samples). (3) With cache_prompt=true, the style sample is cached in KV cache after the first request. (4) User sends draft text requesting style revision. (5) Temperature 0.7 allows creative variation while temperature 0.2 enforces faithful style transfer for formal documents. (6) Response includes both revised text and a 'changes made' explanation. The style-encoding approach in the system prompt achieves better results than LoRA fine-tuning for style transfer at equivalent inference cost because style information can be updated per-session without retraining.

evidence_refs.excerpt: System prompt and cache_prompt for style encoding

statement: llama.cpp uses llama.h as the stable C API surface while the internal C++ implementation changes freely. This separation allows llama-cpp-python, llama-cpp-node, and other language bindings to target a stable ABI without recompiling with every llama.cpp release. The C API exports ~50 functions covering model loading, context creation, tokenization, KV cache operations, and inference. ABI compatibility: llama.cpp increments a version constant (LLAMA_API_VERSION) when the ABI changes, allowing bindings to check compatibility at runtime. The C API is intentionally low-level — it does not include convenience functions like 'run inference and return string', forcing callers to manage the token loop, which enables fine-grained control over streaming and interruption.

evidence_refs.excerpt: C API design rationale and versioning

statement: llama.cpp's --prompt-cache feature saves the KV cache to disk between server restarts, allowing the system prompt to be pre-computed once rather than on every startup. However, this cache is invalidated and regenerated silently whenever the model file, n_ctx, or system prompt changes. The silent invalidation means: after a model update (new quantization, different GGUF), the cached KV states are still read from disk but correspond to the old model's weight configuration, producing garbage output. The server does not validate the model hash against the cache file. Fix: always delete the prompt cache file (--prompt-cache path) when updating the model or changing n_ctx. Set up a model deployment script that removes cache files as part of the upgrade process.

An offline AI-assisted data labeling system with llama.cpp: (1) Label schema defined as a JSON grammar (e.g., {category: 'POSITIVE'|'NEGATIVE'|'NEUTRAL', confidence: 0.0-1.0, reasoning: string}). (2) llama-server runs Llama-3.1-70B-Q4_K_M for high-quality annotations. (3) Python worker pool (8 workers) sends batches of unlabeled items to llama-server /v1/chat/completions with grammar constraint. (4) High-confidence auto-accepted (confidence > 0.9), low-confidence queued for human review. (5) Human-reviewed items used to improve the system prompt with few-shot examples. (6) Annotation throughput: ~200 items/minute on A100, ~30 items/minute on RTX 3090. This pipeline achieves 85-90% auto-acceptance rate for classification tasks, reducing human labeling effort by 85-90% while maintaining >95% label quality.

llama.cpp's --numa flag for NUMA-aware memory allocation requires the numactl package on Linux and does not work on macOS or Windows. On multi-socket servers (2x Xeon with NUMA domains), without --numa, all model weights are allocated in one NUMA node's memory, causing 60-90% remote memory accesses from threads running on the other NUMA node, adding 50-100ns latency per memory access. With --numa distribute, weights are interleaved across NUMA nodes, halving remote accesses. For inference-only servers (no GPU), --numa can improve CPU throughput by 20-40% on multi-socket hardware. Single-socket systems (even with many cores) are not NUMA and do not benefit from this flag.

Installing llama.cpp on Windows with CUDA support: (1) Install Visual Studio 2022 with C++ workload and CUDA Toolkit 12.x. (2) Clone repo: git clone https://github.com/ggerganov/llama.cpp. (3) Open Developer PowerShell for VS 2022. (4) cmake -B build -DLLAMA_CUDA=ON -G 'Visual Studio 17 2022' -A x64 -DCMAKE_CUDA_ARCHITECTURES=89 (for RTX 40xx, adjust for other GPUs). (5) cmake --build build --config Release. (6) Binaries in build/bin/Release/. Alternative: use pre-built CUDA binaries from GitHub releases (llama-bXXXX-bin-win-cuda-cu12.4-x64.zip) to skip compilation entirely. CUDA_ARCHITECTURES values: 75=Turing(RTX20xx), 86=Ampere(RTX30xx), 89=Ada(RTX40xx), 90=Hopper(H100).

llama.cpp's multimodal (LLaVA) inference produces blank or repetitive outputs when the CLIP projector model (mmproj) version does not match the language model version. Both the base LLM GGUF and the mmproj GGUF must come from the same model release — mixing a LLaVA 1.5 mmproj with a LLaVA 1.6 base model causes the image tokens to be projected into the wrong embedding space. Symptom: text-only prompts work correctly but any prompt with an image produces 'The image shows... the image shows... the image shows...' repetition or completely ignores the image content. Always download LLM and mmproj as a matched pair from the same HuggingFace repository commit. Version pinning: store the HuggingFace commit hash alongside both model files.

evidence_refs.excerpt: LLaVA model and projector version matching requirement

statement: llama.cpp's llama-tokenize binary provides a command-line tool for tokenizing text and displaying token IDs, enabling inspection of how a model's tokenizer handles specific inputs without running full inference. This is valuable for: verifying that special tokens (BOS, EOS, system prompt delimiters) are correctly included in generated token sequences, debugging chat template rendering, and counting tokens accurately for context budget planning. Usage: echo 'Hello world' | ./llama-tokenize -m model.gguf shows token IDs and word piece boundaries. Combined with the --show-count flag, it outputs total token count — more accurate than tiktoken approximations for GGUF models, especially for models with large vocabularies or non-Latin scripts.

Implementing a content moderation pipeline with llama.cpp using a two-stage approach: (1) A fast classifier (Llama-Guard-3 8B Q4_K_M or ShieldLlama 7B) screens content in <200ms with binary grammar ('safe'|'unsafe') for obvious violations. (2) Only flagged content (typically 1-5% of traffic) proceeds to a detailed analysis stage with a larger model that categorizes the violation type and severity. (3) Clear violations (confidence > 0.95) are auto-rejected; borderline cases queue for human review. (4) Safe content passes through immediately. This architecture achieves 99%+ throughput reduction for the expensive detailed analysis stage while maintaining safety coverage. The two-stage approach is 10-20x more cost-effective than running all content through the large model.

llama.cpp's RoPE implementation for models using ALiBi (Attention with Linear Biases, used by BLOOM, MPT, Falcon early versions) positional encoding is not fully supported and falls back to standard RoPE. This causes positional encoding mismatches for ALiBi-trained models when loaded in llama.cpp, resulting in length generalization failures — the model works normally for short contexts but degrades past its training length without the ALiBi-expected extrapolation. BLOOM, MPT-7B-chat, and early Falcon models use ALiBi. Diagnosis: check GGUF metadata for attention_head_count_kv vs tokenizer.ggml.padding_token_id; ALiBi models have characteristic metadata. For ALiBi models, use specialized runners (ctransformers) or wait for llama.cpp ALiBi support to be implemented for your target architecture.

llama.cpp implements continuous integration and regression testing via a GitHub Actions matrix that tests all major backends (CUDA, Metal, CPU AVX/AVX2/AVX512, ARM NEON) on every pull request. This is critical because SIMD-optimized quantized matrix multiplication code is highly architecture-specific and easy to break — a change to the Q4_K_M multiplication kernel must be verified to produce identical logit outputs on all supported hardware. The CI system runs inference on a small test model and verifies token outputs match expected values, catching numerical regressions before they reach users. Without this multi-backend CI, llama.cpp's rapid development pace would frequently break users on non-CUDA platforms. Community contributors are explicitly required to not break non-tested backends.

Enabling and using llama.cpp's built-in OpenAI-compatible embedding API: (1) Start server with a dedicated embedding model: llama-server -m nomic-embed-text-v1.5.Q4_K_M.gguf --embedding --pooling mean --port 8081 (separate port from chat model). (2) Test: curl -X POST http://localhost:8081/v1/embeddings -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"model":"nomic","input":"Hello world"}'. (3) Batch embedding: send array of strings in 'input' field, max 2048 tokens total across all inputs. (4) Parse float32 embedding vectors from response.data[].embedding. (5) Normalize: divide each vector by its L2 norm for cosine similarity computation. Configure batch size with --batch-size to optimize throughput: batch-size 512 for short texts, 128 for longer documents. Monitor with /metrics to verify GPU utilization (should be >80% during batch embedding).

llama.cpp's structured output enforcement via GBNF grammars fails for multilingual outputs where the grammar constrains character ranges. A grammar like root ::= [a-zA-Z]+ works for English but rejects valid Chinese/Japanese/Korean output because non-ASCII codepoints are not included in the character class. This causes the model to get stuck: it wants to generate a CJK character but the grammar rejects it, leading to repeated whitespace or punctuation as the only grammatically valid tokens. Fix for multilingual: use permissive character classes in grammars (root ::= .+) and rely on semantic constraints in the prompt, or use token-level filtering instead of character-level grammars for multilingual structured output. The GBNF grammar system was designed for ASCII-centric structured formats (JSON, URLs) and has incomplete Unicode support.

evidence_refs.excerpt: Grammar character class and Unicode limitations

statement: Building a personalized recommendation engine with llama.cpp: (1) User interaction history (viewed items, ratings, purchase history) formatted as structured context within a 4K token window. (2) Product catalog metadata embedded with llama-embedding and indexed in FAISS. (3) Recommendation request: embed the user's recent interactions, retrieve top-50 candidate products via FAISS, construct a prompt with user history + 50 candidates, ask the model to rank and explain recommendations. (4) Grammar-constrained JSON output: {recommendations: [{product_id, score, reason}]}. The explain-ability of LLM recommendations (natural language reasons) provides UX advantages over traditional collaborative filtering. For cold-start users (no history), the model uses demographic and browsing session context instead.

llama.cpp's thread count configuration (--threads for CPU computation, --threads-batch for batch processing) does not benefit from hyperthreading — using thread count equal to physical cores rather than logical threads (hyperthreads) typically produces better performance. On a 16-core/32-thread CPU, --threads 16 outperforms --threads 32 by 15-25% for LLM inference because the memory-bandwidth-bound nature of transformer decode means hyperthreads compete for the same memory bus rather than providing additional compute. GPU-accelerated inference is not affected by this parameter. Optimal thread count: physical_cores - 2 (leave 2 cores for OS and I/O). On AMD CPUs with 3D V-Cache (Ryzen 7X3D), the extra L3 cache changes this rule — --threads equal to full physical core count performs best.

evidence_refs.excerpt: Thread count optimization for CPU inference

statement: Automated code security review pipeline with llama.cpp: (1) Repository scanning with semgrep produces an initial list of potential vulnerabilities with file:line:code triples. (2) For each finding, llama-server (CodeLlama-34B-Q4_K_M) receives the vulnerable code snippet, semgrep rule description, and 100-line context window, and is asked to: (a) confirm if the finding is a true positive, (b) assess severity, (c) generate a code fix. Grammar enforces JSON output: {is_true_positive: bool, severity: 'CRITICAL'|'HIGH'|'MEDIUM'|'LOW', fix: string, explanation: string}. (3) True positives with generated fixes create GitHub Issues with auto-assigned severity labels. (4) False positive rate with CodeLlama-34B is ~15% versus semgrep alone at ~40%, reducing engineer triage time by 60%.

llama.cpp's HTTP server crashes with a 'too many open files' error under sustained high load when connections are not properly closed by clients. Each request opens a socket file descriptor; if clients leave connections in TIME_WAIT state (common with naive HTTP clients that don't set Connection: close or don't reuse connections via keep-alive properly), the server accumulates file descriptors until hitting the OS limit (default 1024 on many Linux systems). Fix: increase system file descriptor limit (ulimit -n 65536 in systemd service) and set --timeout 60 to aggressively close idle connections. Client-side: use HTTP connection pooling (requests.Session in Python) to reuse connections rather than opening a new connection per request. Monitor with lsof -p $(pgrep llama-server) | wc -l.

evidence_refs.excerpt: File descriptor limits and connection management

statement: Building llama.cpp WebAssembly (WASM) for browser-based inference: (1) Install Emscripten: git clone https://github.com/emscripten-core/emsdk && emsdk install latest && emsdk activate latest. (2) Source environment: source emsdk_env.sh. (3) cmake -B build-wasm -DLLAMA_WASM_SIMD=ON -DCMAKE_TOOLCHAIN_FILE=$EMSDK/upstream/emscripten/cmake/Modules/Platform/Emscripten.cmake. (4) cmake --build build-wasm. (5) Deploy .wasm + .js files to web server. Browser constraints: no CUDA/Metal/Vulkan (CPU only), max ~4GB heap (browser 32-bit address space for WASM on some browsers), limited threading via SharedArrayBuffer (requires COOP/COEP headers). Practical models: Q4_K_M 7B (3.8GB) fits in 4GB WASM heap on Chrome/Firefox with SIMD enabled. Use case: offline AI assistant apps that run entirely in browser without backend infrastructure.

llama.cpp's --grp-attn-n flag for self-extend (context length extension via grouped attention) causes output quality regression when set too aggressively. Self-extend maps tokens beyond the training context length to positions within the training context using grouped attention, enabling inference at 2-4× training length without fine-tuning. However, --grp-attn-n 4 (grouping every 4 positions into 1) with --grp-attn-w 512 (context window for ungrouped attention) at 16K context on a 4K-trained model produces clearly degraded coherence compared to a model properly fine-tuned for 16K. The quality gap increases with context ratio: 2× training length is acceptable, 4× is borderline, 8× is generally too degraded for production use. Self-extend should be viewed as a workaround for occasional long inputs, not a general-purpose long-context solution.

evidence_refs.excerpt: Self-extend grouped attention for context extension

statement: llama.cpp's /tokenize endpoint on llama-server converts text to token IDs and returns them as a JSON array, enabling client-side token counting without running inference. This is useful for: (1) pre-validating that prompts fit within n_ctx before sending generation requests, (2) implementing token-based pricing for API usage metering, (3) analyzing tokenization patterns for prompt engineering. The endpoint accepts any text including partial tokens and returns the complete token sequence the model would see. Unlike tiktoken (which approximates tokenization for non-OpenAI models), /tokenize uses the model's actual GGUF-embedded tokenizer, providing exact token counts for correct chunk sizing in RAG pipelines.

llama.cpp's GGUF model format does not support streaming model loading during inference — the full model must be loaded into memory before the first token can be generated. For a 70B Q4_K_M model (40GB), loading takes 40-120 seconds depending on storage I/O speed. This cold-start latency is a fundamental constraint of the mmap-based loading approach: mmap maps the file into virtual address space instantly but actual page loading happens on first access (page faults during the first forward pass). Mitigation: pre-warm the model with a dummy request after startup (before routing real traffic), use fast NVMe storage, or enable --mlock to keep the model resident in RAM after the first load (prevents subsequent cold starts at the cost of pinned RAM).

Implementing a database query assistant with llama.cpp using schema-constrained SQL generation: (1) llama-server serves a code model (Codestral-22B or DeepSeek-Coder-33B) with a grammar that enforces valid SQL syntax for the target dialect (PostgreSQL, SQLite). (2) System prompt encodes the database schema: table names, column names, types, and example queries. (3) User's natural language question is sent as the user message. (4) Grammar constrains output to valid SQL SELECT statements (no DDL/DML to prevent injection). (5) Generated SQL is executed against a read-only database connection with query timeout. (6) Results returned to the model for natural language summary. Grammar prevents SQL injection by construction: the GBNF grammar only allows SELECT, WHERE, JOIN, GROUP BY — no INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, DROP. This provides structural injection prevention beyond parameterized queries.

llama.cpp inference on Windows with AMD GPU via ROCm fails because ROCm (AMD's CUDA equivalent) has very limited Windows support — ROCm 5.x only supports a small subset of AMD GPUs on Windows and requires specific Windows SDK versions. Most users find ROCm on Windows non-functional and must use Vulkan backend instead. For AMD GPU on Windows: build with -DLLAMA_VULKAN=ON and install the Vulkan SDK from LunarG. The Vulkan backend on AMD Windows achieves 60-80% of theoretical VRAM bandwidth, making it viable for production use. ROCm Windows support is expected to improve in ROCm 6.x but as of ROCm 5.7 it is not recommended for production inference on Windows.

evidence_refs.excerpt: AMD GPU backend selection for Windows

statement: llama.cpp does not implement batched decoding (generating multiple independent completion sequences in a single forward pass from different starting points), unlike vLLM's PagedAttention or TGI's token merging. This is because llama.cpp's design prioritizes single-request latency over throughput: a user running locally wants the fastest possible response to their single query, not maximum batch throughput. For production high-throughput serving (hundreds of requests/second), vLLM or TGI are more appropriate. llama.cpp's --parallel flag enables concurrent request processing by interleaving tokens from different sequences in the same batch, which is continuous batching, but each request still sees its own isolated KV cache — not the batched speculative decoding that vLLM implements.

evidence_refs.excerpt: llama.cpp focus on single-request inference efficiency

statement: Updating llama.cpp to a new release while preserving production service continuity: (1) Read the CHANGELOG for the new release, noting any breaking changes to CLI flags, API endpoints, or GGUF format requirements. (2) Test new binary against existing model with llama-bench and perplexity check. (3) If GGUF format changed (new metadata fields required), re-convert or re-download models. (4) Stage deployment: update the non-primary server instance first (port 8081). (5) Run smoke tests via curl against 8081. (6) If tests pass, apply blue-green switch. Rollback plan: keep previous binary at /usr/local/bin/llama-server.prev. Breaking changes to watch: --parallel became --n-parallel in some versions, API endpoint paths changed in b2700+, grammar syntax changed for nested rules. Always read release notes for 'breaking' keyword.

llama.cpp's support for bfloat16 (BF16) weights is limited — BF16 is not a quantization format in GGUF (which uses F16 as the float format baseline). Models converted from bfloat16 safetensors are automatically converted to float16 during GGUF conversion, which involves a round-trip through float32 to preserve precision. This conversion is lossless for weights within the F16 range, but models that intentionally use bfloat16's wider dynamic range (some large models use BF16 because it handles large weight values better than F16) may experience subtle quality degradation after conversion. In practice, the quality difference is negligible for inference (BF16 vs F16 perplexity difference < 0.01 ppl), but users should be aware when reporting performance gaps versus BF16 reference implementations.

evidence_refs.excerpt: BF16 to F16 conversion during model loading

statement: llama.cpp supports sampling with a structured mirostat algorithm (mirostat v2 via --mirostat 2 --mirostat-tau 5.0 --mirostat-eta 0.1) that dynamically adjusts temperature to maintain a target perplexity level throughout generation. Unlike fixed temperature, mirostat produces consistently coherent text regardless of how the model's probability distribution evolves mid-generation: when the model is very confident (low entropy), mirostat decreases effective temperature to prevent random divergence; when the model is uncertain (high entropy), it increases temperature to prevent getting stuck. For open-ended creative writing where coherence is paramount, mirostat v2 outperforms fixed temperature sampling, producing more sustained narrative quality. The tau parameter (target surprise level) typically ranges 3-7 for most tasks.

An automated podcast transcription and episode search system with llama.cpp: (1) whisper.cpp transcribes podcast episodes to timestamped SRT files. (2) SRT files chunked into 30-second segments with timestamps preserved. (3) llama-embedding generates embeddings for each chunk. (4) Embeddings stored in SQLite with FTS5 for hybrid search (semantic + keyword). (5) A search interface sends queries to both semantic search (llama-embedding query vector vs stored embeddings) and keyword FTS5 search, combining results with reciprocal rank fusion. (6) Top results include playback links with timestamps. (7) A LLM-generated episode summary (llama-server) for each episode enables browsing without listening. This pipeline converts a podcast library into a searchable knowledge base, processing one hour of audio in ~10 minutes.

evidence_refs.excerpt: whisper.cpp SRT output for transcription pipelines

statement: llama.cpp's JSON grammar generation from JSON Schema (grammar-from-json-schema binary) does not support all JSON Schema keywords. Unsupported: allOf, anyOf, oneOf (logical combinators), if/then/else (conditional schemas), dependencies, patternProperties with complex regex. Supported: type, properties, required, enum, const, array items, minLength/maxLength (for string constraints in newer versions). When an unsupported keyword is encountered, grammar-from-json-schema may silently ignore it and generate a permissive grammar that allows values the schema intended to reject. Always test grammar-constrained generation with boundary values to verify the grammar actually enforces the intended constraints, especially for schemas with union types or conditional validation.

evidence_refs.excerpt: JSON Schema to GBNF grammar conversion limitations

statement: llama.cpp's server crashes with a segmentation fault on the first inference request when --flash-attn is enabled alongside --cache-type-k f32 (forcing float32 KV cache). Flash Attention implementation assumes half-precision (F16) KV cache tensors for its optimized kernel, but --cache-type-k f32 overrides this to float32. The mismatch between expected and actual tensor types causes an out-of-bounds memory access in the Flash Attention kernel. This combination of flags is nonsensical (Flash Attention already saves memory better than F32 KV cache provides numeric stability) but produces a crash rather than an error message. Workaround: remove --cache-type-k f32 when using --flash-attn, or remove --flash-attn. The bug has been reported and may be fixed in newer releases.

evidence_refs.excerpt: Flash attention and f32 KV cache conflict

statement: Using llama.cpp for zero-shot data transformation and normalization: given inconsistent data formats (dates as '01/15/2024', 'January 15th, 2024', '2024-01-15', '15 Jan 24'), use llama-server with a grammar that enforces ISO 8601 output format (root ::= [0-9]{4} '-' [01][0-9] '-' [0-3][0-9]) to normalize all variants to YYYY-MM-DD. Temperature 0 for consistency. Batch processing: send 50-100 data points per request in a structured format, receive normalized batch as JSON array. This approach handles edge cases that regex cannot: '14th of February 2024', 'the day after Valentine's Day 2024', '2/29/24' (leap year validation). Processing speed: ~1000 records/minute on 7B model. Quality: 98%+ accuracy for common date formats, 90%+ for ambiguous inputs where the model makes reasonable assumptions.

llama.cpp uses GitHub as both the development platform and the binary distribution channel, with pre-built binaries attached as release assets to each GitHub release. This choice means users must have internet access to download binaries but avoids the complexity of maintaining a separate package registry (npm, apt, brew) for each platform combination. The release automation uses GitHub Actions with matrix builds: each platform/backend combination is compiled and the artifact uploaded to the same release. Users can pin to a specific release by downloading from a fixed GitHub release URL, enabling reproducible deployments without requiring local build infrastructure. Enterprise deployments mirror the GitHub releases to internal artifact registries for air-gapped environments.

llama.cpp's llama-cli with --interactive mode fails to handle EOF (Ctrl+D) gracefully on Windows — instead of cleanly exiting the conversation loop, it enters an infinite loop consuming 100% CPU. This is a Windows readline handling edge case where the Windows console does not send EOF to stdin the same way Unix terminals do. Workaround on Windows: use Ctrl+C to terminate the interactive session instead of Ctrl+D, or use llama-server REST API rather than interactive mode for Windows deployments. The llama-server binary handles Windows signals correctly and is the recommended production interface on Windows. The interactive CLI mode is primarily designed for Unix-like systems.

llama.cpp supports the Gemma and Gemma 2 model architectures, including Gemma-2-27B which uses a novel interleaved local+global attention pattern. The Gemma 2 architecture is noteworthy because it achieves near-GPT-4-level performance at 27B parameters due to the interleaved attention pattern and knowledge distillation training. llama.cpp implements the local+global attention efficiently, with the local attention windows processed on CPU to save VRAM and global attention on GPU. Gemma 2 27B at Q4_K_M (14.8GB) fits on a 16GB GPU, making it the highest-quality model that fits in 16GB VRAM as of 2024. Configuration: use --n-gpu-layers 99 and ensure the GGUF from google/gemma-2-27b-it is used (not the base variant).

evidence_refs.excerpt: Gemma 2 architecture support and interleaved attention

statement: Setting up a development environment for contributing to llama.cpp: (1) Fork and clone: git clone https://github.com/YOUR_FORK/llama.cpp. (2) Install dependencies: sudo apt install cmake build-essential git (Linux) or xcode-select --install (macOS). (3) Build with debug symbols: cmake -B build -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Debug -DLLAMA_SANITIZE_ADDRESS=ON -DLLAMA_SANITIZE_UNDEFINED=ON. (4) Run tests: ctest --test-dir build -L 'main|simple|tokenizer' to run fast tests. (5) Test your change: ./build/bin/llama-cli -m /path/to/small-model.gguf -p 'Hi' -n 5. (6) Check code style: clang-format --dry-run src/your_file.cpp. (7) Run CI tests locally with act (GitHub Actions local runner). AddressSanitizer catches memory bugs immediately rather than randomly during production use.

llama.cpp's embedding normalization with --pooling cls produces incorrect embeddings for models that use pooling over the last non-padding token rather than the first token (CLS position). This is a common source of confusion: BERT and its derivatives use CLS token pooling (first token), while some newer embedding models (E5, BGE) use either mean pooling or last-token pooling. Using cls pooling for a mean-pooling model produces embeddings from only one token position, dramatically reducing embedding quality. Cosine similarity between semantically similar texts drops from >0.8 to 0.3-0.5, causing retrieval failures. Always verify the correct pooling type from the model card before deployment. The safest default for unknown models is mean pooling.

evidence_refs.excerpt: Pooling type selection for different embedding models

statement: A local AI research assistant for scientific literature review with llama.cpp: (1) ArXiv paper PDFs extracted to text with pdfminer, chunked at section boundaries (Introduction, Methods, Results, Discussion). (2) nomic-embed-text GGUF generates 768-dim embeddings for each section. (3) Embeddings stored in Qdrant with metadata: paper_id, section, authors, year. (4) Daily update job fetches new papers from ArXiv API in target categories. (5) Query pipeline: embed user's research question, retrieve top-10 sections across top-50 papers, assemble into a prompt. (6) Llama-3.1-70B generates a literature review paragraph with section-level citations. (7) Citation verification: check that quoted text appears in the source sections (prevents hallucinated citations). This pipeline processes 500 new papers per day and maintains a searchable corpus of 50,000+ papers on a single server.

evidence_refs.excerpt: Document processing pipeline with embedding and generation

statement: llama.cpp's llama-server does not implement authentication for the /metrics endpoint even when --api-key is set. The Prometheus metrics endpoint at /metrics exposes potentially sensitive operational data: model name, request rates, queue depths, and performance metrics that could reveal infrastructure topology to unauthorized parties. In security-conscious deployments, /metrics should be accessible only from the monitoring server's IP. Fix: deploy llama-server behind an nginx reverse proxy that requires authentication for /metrics but allows unauthenticated access to /v1/* for API clients. Or use network-level access control (firewall rules) to restrict /metrics to the monitoring subnet. This is a known gap in llama-server's security model as of 2024.

evidence_refs.excerpt: Authentication and security configuration for server

statement: Continuous model quality monitoring with llama.cpp in production: (1) Maintain a golden test suite of 100 prompts with known expected outputs (deterministic at temperature=0). (2) Run the golden test suite against every new server deployment before routing live traffic. (3) Calculate semantic similarity between actual outputs and expected outputs using cosine similarity of embeddings. (4) Alert if average similarity drops below 0.85 (threshold calibrated during baseline establishment). (5) Monitor production output quality by sampling 1% of live responses through the same embedding similarity check against prompt-type baselines. This provides continuous quality assurance beyond latency/error-rate monitoring, catching quality regressions that don't manifest as HTTP errors — for example, a wrong chat template causing semantically incorrect but syntactically valid responses.

llama.cpp fails to load Q6_K quantized models with very large layer counts (>100 layers, e.g., hypothetical 200B dense models) due to a 32-bit integer overflow in the tensor offset calculation during GGUF parsing. Tensor data offsets in GGUF are stored as uint64 but an internal calculation uses int32 arithmetic when computing cumulative offsets, overflowing for models where the cumulative offset exceeds 2GB at certain layer positions. This bug was present before b3100 and affects only extreme models. The workaround is to use F16 or Q8_0 quantization (different tensor packing that avoids the overflow) or update to a patched llama.cpp version. Most practical models (up to 70B) do not exceed the overflow threshold.

evidence_refs.excerpt: Integer overflow in large model tensor offset calculation

statement: llama.cpp's llama-simple example demonstrates minimal inference in <200 lines of C++ code, making it the recommended starting point for embedding llama.cpp into C++ applications. The example shows the essential API flow: llama_model_load_from_file → llama_new_context_with_model → llama_tokenize → llama_batch_add → llama_decode → llama_sampling_sample → llama_sampling_accept, with proper resource cleanup. For application developers embedding inference into games, desktop apps, or embedded systems, forking llama-simple and adding application-specific logic is more maintainable than integrating the full llama-server HTTP stack. The example is tested on every llama.cpp release to ensure the core API remains stable.

evidence_refs.excerpt: Minimal C++ API example for application integration

statement: llama.cpp's sampler types are modular and composable rather than parameterized switches, following a pipeline design where each sampler takes the logit distribution and returns a (possibly modified) distribution or a sampled token. This enables novel sampler combinations not anticipated by the original designers: min-p + tfs + typical can be chained in any order, and custom samplers can be added as plugins. The modular design came from community contributions exploring DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself penalty), XTC (Extreme Token Censoring), and other experimental samplers that were added to llama.cpp via PRs. A sampler pipeline is more extensible than a parameter-based system (like HuggingFace's GenerationConfig) where adding a new sampler requires modifying the core generation function.

Diagnosing and fixing slow first-token latency (TTFT) in llama-server: (1) Measure TTFT vs prompt length: if TTFT scales linearly with prompt length, prefill is the bottleneck; if TTFT is constant, it's decode-bound (unusual for first token). (2) For prefill bottleneck on GPU: increase --batch-size (default 512) to 2048 or 4096 to process more tokens per forward pass — this alone can improve TTFT 3-5x for long prompts. (3) For prefill bottleneck on CPU: enable AVX512 if available (cmake -DLLAMA_AVX512=ON), or add GPU offloading for the attention layers (--n-gpu-layers 16 adds GPU attention while keeping FFN on CPU). (4) Use cache_prompt to skip re-encoding shared system prompts — the biggest TTFT win for conversational applications. (5) Monitor with curl including request timestamps and compare to /metrics latency histogram.

llama.cpp's grammar-constrained generation degrades in quality when the grammar is very restrictive and the model has low probability mass on grammar-valid tokens. The grammar parser masks out invalid tokens by setting their logits to -inf, leaving only valid tokens with nonzero probability. If the model's top-1 token (most likely based on context) is always masked out, generation quality degrades: the model's 'real' next token is never produced, and the constrained output is essentially a forced substitution. Symptom: structured output that is syntactically valid but semantically wrong (wrong field values, out-of-context answers). Detection: compare logprob of constrained output versus unconstrained output — a >3 logprob difference indicates significant grammar constraint forcing. Fix: make grammar more permissive, use bigger models, or use post-generation parsing instead.

evidence_refs.excerpt: Grammar constraint quality impact on generation

statement: Real-time meeting assistant with llama.cpp: (1) whisper.cpp in streaming mode captures audio from microphone in 5-second chunks and transcribes each chunk. (2) A rolling buffer maintains the last 10 minutes of transcription (approximately 2000 tokens). (3) llama-server receives the buffer + user query (e.g., 'What were the main points of the last 3 minutes?'). (4) Response generated in <5 seconds on RTX 3090 with 8B model. (5) Auto-generate a structured action item list after each explicit topic change detected in the transcript. (6) Keyword-triggered summaries: 'let's recap' triggers an automatic 3-bullet summary. Privacy: all processing is local, audio never leaves the device. This pipeline requires a dedicated machine (meeting quality + inference simultaneously) — a MacBook Pro M3 Max handles this with headroom.

llama.cpp's context size (n_ctx) set at server startup is a global fixed limit — it cannot be changed between requests without restarting the server. If n_ctx=4096 and a request needs 8192 tokens context, the server truncates the input and returns a degraded response (or an error in strict mode). Planning: set n_ctx to the 99th percentile expected request size, not the maximum possible, because larger n_ctx reserves more KV cache memory per slot. With n_ctx=32768 and --parallel 4: 32768 × 4 × n_layers × head_dim × 2 bytes = potentially >40GB KV cache memory for a 70B model — larger than the model weights themselves. For variable-length workloads, deploy multiple server instances with different n_ctx values and route requests based on estimated prompt length.

evidence_refs.excerpt: n_ctx memory implications and fixed configuration

statement: Using llama.cpp for automated test case generation in software testing: (1) Feed function signature, docstring, and implementation to llama-server with system prompt 'You are a software tester. Generate pytest test cases including happy path, edge cases, and error conditions.' (2) Grammar enforces valid Python test function syntax starting with def test_. (3) Auto-execute generated tests with pytest -x (fail-fast) to filter immediately-failing tests. (4) Surviving tests added to the test suite with human review queued for non-trivial cases. (5) Weekly, generate mutation tests (modify existing tests to check they actually detect bugs). On a Python project of 10,000 functions, this pipeline generates 50,000+ test cases in 8 hours with 60-70% immediate pass rate, achieving 80% code coverage from zero in one automated run. The 30-40% failing tests still serve as documentation of expected behavior.

llama.cpp installed via Homebrew (brew install llama.cpp) provides binaries compiled for the generic CPU target without GPU acceleration. Users who install via Homebrew and expect Metal GPU acceleration on Apple Silicon are disappointed to find CPU-only performance. The Homebrew formula compiles with -DLLAMA_METAL=ON but the resulting binary may have reduced Metal performance due to Homebrew's generic optimization flags. For production Metal performance, compile from source with -DLLAMA_NATIVE=ON -DLLAMA_METAL=ON. The Homebrew version is appropriate for quick evaluation and development where maximum performance is not required. Check if Metal is active: llama-cli --version shows metal in the build string for properly Metal-enabled builds.

evidence_refs.excerpt: Homebrew vs. source build performance differences

statement: llama.cpp's gguf-dump utility provides human-readable inspection of any GGUF file's metadata, tensor list, and structural information without loading the model for inference. Key use cases: (1) Verify model architecture (llama, mistral, gemma, phi) before loading, (2) Check chat template for instruct models, (3) Count total parameters from tensor shapes, (4) Verify quantization type of each tensor, (5) Inspect special token IDs (BOS, EOS, PAD, tool call tokens). Usage: python3 gguf-py/scripts/gguf_dump.py model.gguf --no-tensors (metadata only, fast) or without --no-tensors for full tensor list. This enables model provenance verification and debugging without requiring a GPU or model load, making it useful in CI pipelines for model artifact validation.

evidence_refs.excerpt: gguf-dump for model inspection and verification

statement: Configuring llama.cpp for maximum throughput on a single A100 80GB GPU: (1) Load largest possible model: Llama-3.1-70B-Q4_K_M (40GB) with --n-gpu-layers 99 --flash-attn. (2) --ctx-size 8192 × --parallel 8 = 65K total context slots (requires ~32GB KV cache at 16 layers). Actually limit: --ctx-size 4096 --parallel 16 for better throughput. (3) Enable continuous batching: --cont-batching. (4) Maximize batch size: --batch-size 4096. (5) Disable output sampling for throughput benchmark: --no-mmap for consistent latency. (6) Monitor: GET /metrics and watch generated_tokens_per_second. Expected throughput: 200-400 tokens/sec aggregate across 16 parallel requests versus 50-80 tokens/sec single request. The GPU processes token batches from all parallel requests in a single kernel launch, achieving near-100% GPU utilization.

llama.cpp's RoPE frequency scaling for context extension silently reduces embedding quality for retrieval tasks. Models like Llama-3.1 with 128K context use RoPE base frequency 500000 rather than the standard 10000. When llama-embedding is run with the same model for embedding generation, the extended RoPE positions cause positional embeddings of semantically similar tokens to diverge more than with the original 10000 base, because the RoPE frequencies are tuned for generation not retrieval. Embedding models should use rope_freq_base appropriate to their training, not the generation model's extended rope settings. A dedicated embedding model (nomic-embed-text, E5-mistral) trained specifically for retrieval will outperform a generation model with extended RoPE for embedding tasks.

evidence_refs.excerpt: RoPE settings and embedding quality interaction

statement: A local AI pair programmer with context-awareness across files using llama.cpp: (1) A file watcher tracks changes to project files and maintains an embedding index of all source files (re-embeds changed files incrementally). (2) When a user writes a comment like '# TODO: implement the authentication check here', the IDE plugin extracts the surrounding context (100 lines) and queries the embedding index for relevant files (auth.py, user.py, config.py). (3) Top-3 relevant files retrieved and concatenated as context (up to 8K tokens). (4) llama-server generates implementation code at the TODO position via /infill endpoint. (5) User accepts, rejects, or modifies the suggestion. (6) Accepted suggestions incrementally fine-tune the local model via LoRA (nightly batch). This architecture learns the project's coding style over time, improving suggestion quality with each accepted suggestion.

evidence_refs.excerpt: FIM with multi-file context for coding assistant

statement: llama.cpp's CUDA backend requires compute capability 6.0 (Pascal architecture) as the minimum GPU requirement. GPUs older than Pascal (Maxwell: GTX 900 series, Kepler: GTX 700 series) do not support the CUDA 11+ kernels that llama.cpp uses and produce compilation or runtime errors. The minimum practical GPU for production inference is GTX 1060 6GB (Pascal, CC 6.1), which can run Q4_K_M 7B models at 10-15 tokens/sec — sufficient for personal use but not production serving. For production, RTX 20xx (Turing, CC 7.5) adds Tensor Cores for FP16 matmul acceleration, roughly doubling throughput versus Pascal at the same VRAM. CUDA compute capabilities: Volta=7.0 (V100), Turing=7.5 (RTX 20xx), Ampere=8.0-8.6 (RTX 30xx), Ada=8.9 (RTX 40xx), Hopper=9.0 (H100).

Implementing a document clustering pipeline with llama.cpp: (1) llama-embedding generates embeddings for all documents (batched for efficiency). (2) K-means clustering groups documents by semantic similarity (scikit-learn). (3) For each cluster centroid, find the N nearest documents. (4) Send cluster sample to llama-server: 'Given these document excerpts, generate a concise cluster label and 3-sentence description'. (5) Grammar enforces JSON output: {label: string, description: string, keywords: string[]}. (6) Cluster labels and descriptions saved to database for browsing interface. This pipeline enables automatic taxonomy generation for large document collections without predefined categories. Quality: cluster labels are 85-90% meaningful to domain experts, significantly reducing time to create a browsable document taxonomy. Works for corpora of 10,000-1,000,000 documents.

llama.cpp's model conversion script convert_hf_to_gguf.py fails with 'KeyError: q_proj' for models that use non-standard attention projection naming (e.g., c_attn for GPT-2 style models where Q, K, V are concatenated in a single weight). The converter expects attention weights named query_key_value or q_proj/k_proj/v_proj separately; concatenated QKV weights require a custom split operation before conversion. Fix: write a pre-processing script that splits the concatenated weight: qkv = model.transformer.h[i].attn.c_attn.weight; q, k, v = qkv.split(n_embd, dim=0). This affects: GPT-2 family, MPT, BLOOM attention blocks, and some custom models. Architectures built for HuggingFace transformers using the standard Llama/Mistral naming convention (q_proj, k_proj, v_proj) convert without issues.

llama.cpp's /v1/chat/completions endpoint supports logit_bias parameter, enabling per-token probability adjustments to guide or constrain generation without full grammar constraints. Setting logit_bias for specific token IDs shifts their logit values: positive values increase probability (bias toward that token), negative values decrease it (bias away). Use cases: (1) Prevent brand names from appearing in outputs by biasing their token IDs to -100 (effectively banned), (2) Enforce language by biasing against common tokens of other languages, (3) Create gentle stylistic preferences by applying small positive biases (+2 to +5) to formal/informal register tokens. Unlike grammar constraints that hard-block tokens, logit_bias allows nuanced probability shaping while maintaining generation fluency.

llama.cpp does not implement a memory-efficient paged KV cache (unlike vLLM's PagedAttention) because the design philosophy prioritizes simplicity and low latency for single-user inference over maximum throughput for concurrent users. Paged KV caches reduce memory fragmentation for variable-length sequences in high-concurrency scenarios (100+ simultaneous requests), but add complexity: page tables, page fault handling, and memory compaction logic. For llama.cpp's target use case (personal deployment, up to 16 parallel requests), static KV allocation provides predictable latency without the overhead of page management. Users needing PagedAttention efficiency (>50 concurrent users, variable-length generation) should use vLLM or TGI, which accept this complexity trade-off for throughput gains.

evidence_refs.excerpt: llama.cpp design philosophy and use case scope

statement: Implementing rate limiting for llama-server using nginx as a reverse proxy: (1) nginx config: limit_req_zone $binary_remote_addr zone=llm:10m rate=6r/m; (6 requests per minute per IP). (2) In location block: limit_req zone=llm burst=3 nodelay; returns 429 when limit exceeded. (3) For API key-based rate limiting: extract API key from Authorization header, use $http_authorization as the zone key instead of $binary_remote_addr. (4) Set X-RateLimit-Remaining header with limit_req_status 429 handling. (5) Implement per-user token budget tracking in Redis: increment by estimated token count per request, reject when budget exceeded. This nginx-based approach adds rate limiting to llama-server without modifying the binary, and is maintained independently of llama.cpp updates.

evidence_refs.excerpt: Reverse proxy configuration for production deployment

statement: llama.cpp's --keep flag (preserve N tokens from the initial prompt when context shifting) interacts unexpectedly with system prompts containing special BOS tokens. If --keep 128 is set to preserve the system prompt, but the system prompt includes a 256-token preamble before the user-relevant content, the model may lose the most important part of the system prompt (the actual instructions) while retaining only the BOS token and first 127 tokens of boilerplate. Count tokens precisely: use the /tokenize endpoint to measure the exact token count of your system prompt, then set --keep to that value plus a 10% buffer. Always test context shifting by running a conversation long enough to trigger a shift and verifying the system prompt is still being followed in subsequent responses.

evidence_refs.excerpt: --keep flag for system prompt preservation during context shift

statement: llama.cpp's Stochastic Beam Search implementation (--n-beams N flag) enables beam search decoding as an alternative to autoregressive sampling, generating N parallel hypothesis sequences and selecting the highest-probability complete sequence. For tasks with objectively correct answers (translation, code generation, factual Q&A), beam search (N=4 to 8) achieves higher accuracy than sampling but is N× slower and N× more memory-intensive. Beam search is incompatible with streaming (must complete all beams before returning any output) and grammar constraints (because beam paths may diverge mid-constraint). Best used for offline batch tasks where generation quality matters more than latency: document translation, formal code generation, structured data extraction where the best-of-N selection provides meaningful quality improvement.

llama.cpp cannot perform training, fine-tuning (full parameter), or gradient computation — it is an inference-only framework. The llama-finetune binary is a limited exception that implements LoRA training using the forward pass only (no backpropagation through the main model) by using the small LoRA adapter as the trainable component. For full fine-tuning, RLHF, or DPO training, dedicated training frameworks (HuggingFace Transformers + PEFT, axolotl, LLaMA-Factory) are required. The typical workflow: train/fine-tune in Python with HuggingFace → convert to GGUF with convert_hf_to_gguf.py → deploy for inference with llama.cpp. llama.cpp's role is exclusively inference, and its entire optimization surface (quantization, KV cache, SIMD kernels) is designed for inference performance.

Building a product description generator for e-commerce with llama.cpp: (1) Product catalog ingested as structured JSON: {sku, name, category, attributes, price}. (2) llama-server runs Mistral-7B-Instruct Q4_K_M. (3) For each product, construct a prompt with product attributes and category-specific writing guidelines. (4) Grammar enforces JSON output: {title: string (max 80 chars), description: string (150-300 words), bullet_points: string[] (5 items), seo_keywords: string[]}. (5) Generated content reviewed by automated quality checker (length validation, keyword density, prohibited words). (6) Approved content uploaded to product catalog API. Processing speed: 1000 products/hour on RTX 3090. Cost: $0.00/product (versus $0.05-0.20/product with API providers at scale). ROI breakeven: ~5000 product catalog.

llama.cpp's multi-GPU tensor split does not support asymmetric GPU configurations where GPUs have different VRAM sizes without manual configuration. With --tensor-split 0,0 (automatic equal split), llama.cpp divides tensors equally between GPUs, causing VRAM overflow on the smaller GPU. For a 24GB + 8GB GPU pair, --tensor-split 0.75,0.25 must be specified explicitly to allocate 75% of tensors to the larger GPU. Without explicit split ratios, the auto-split produces an OOM crash at startup. Calculate the appropriate split ratio as larger_GPU_vram / total_vram. Also note: --tensor-split applies to model weight tensors only — KV cache is allocated separately on each GPU proportional to its tensor count. Monitor actual VRAM usage with nvidia-smi to tune split ratios iteratively.

evidence_refs.excerpt: Tensor split configuration for asymmetric multi-GPU

statement: llama.cpp exposes detailed timing statistics per inference request when --timings flag is set, reporting time-to-first-token (ms), tokens/second during decode, prompt evaluation time, and load time. This enables per-request SLA monitoring and performance regression detection. The timing breakdown distinguishes: (1) load time (model initialization, one-time at startup), (2) prompt eval time (prefill, scales with prompt length), (3) eval time (decode, scales with generated tokens). For production monitoring, these per-request timings can be extracted from llama-server logs with --log-format json and aggregated with a log processor (fluentd, logstash) into Grafana dashboards. P99 latency monitoring catches tail latency regressions that average metrics miss.

Implementing a local grammar and style checker using llama.cpp: (1) Text is sent in 500-word chunks to llama-server with a specialized system prompt for grammar and style analysis. (2) Grammar enforces JSON output: {issues: [{type: 'grammar'|'style'|'clarity', text: string, suggestion: string, position: int}]}. (3) Issues with position offsets are highlighted in the editor. (4) One-click 'accept all grammar fixes' applies all issue.suggestion values. (5) Style analysis includes: passive voice detection, sentence length variation, readability score. (6) Calibration: a small correction history model (fine-tuned on accepted/rejected corrections) personalizes suggestions to the user's writing style. Quality: 85-90% issue detection rate versus Grammarly for grammar errors, with stronger performance on technical writing (jargon-aware vs Grammarly's general-purpose model).

llama.cpp's speculative decoding fails when the draft model has a different vocabulary from the main model, even if both are based on the same base architecture. Fine-tuned models that added special tokens to the vocabulary (common for domain-specific models with task tokens like <SUMMARIZE>, <CODE>) have a different token ID space than the base model. When the draft model proposes token 12800 (<SUMMARIZE>) and the main model's vocabulary maps ID 12800 to a different token ('▁naturally'), the verification step incorrectly rejects valid proposed tokens. Prevention: only use speculative decoding with draft models from the same model family with identical base vocabularies. Check vocabulary size match: gguf-dump draft.gguf | grep tokenizer.ggml.tokens | wc vs main.gguf should be equal.

A comprehensive AI-powered customer feedback analysis pipeline with llama.cpp: (1) Customer feedback collected from multiple sources (email, in-app, social media) and stored in PostgreSQL. (2) Daily batch job processes new feedback: llama-server classifies sentiment (positive/neutral/negative), extracts product feature mentions, and identifies actionable feature requests. (3) Grammar constraints ensure consistent JSON output: {sentiment: string, score: int, features_mentioned: string[], is_feature_request: bool, priority: 'high'|'medium'|'low', summary: string}. (4) Classified feedback stored back to database. (5) Weekly summary report: llama-server generates executive summary of top themes, trend analysis, and priority feature requests. (6) Integration with project management API auto-creates tickets for high-priority feature requests. Processing 10,000 feedback items/day at <$0.001/item operational cost (electricity only), enabling comprehensive VoC analysis that was previously cost-prohibitive.
